AI.tldr: The paper proposes KM-ARL and KM-ADD, non-parametric estimators for average run length and average detection delay in quickest changepoint detection, drawing on survival analysis to handle limited and irregular sequence lengths, with proven asymptotic unbiasedness and demonstrated practical utility.
AI.motivation: ARL and ADD are standard optimality criteria in QCD but cannot be reliably applied to real-world datasets due to finite and irregular sequence lengths. The authors aim to bridge this gap.
AI.method: The authors draw an analogy between QCD and survival analysis, using Kaplan-Meier estimators to model detection probabilities under sequence truncation. They derive bias bounds and prove asymptotic unbiasedness.
AI.result: Experiments on simulated and real-world datasets show the estimators enhance robustness against limited/irregular sequence lengths, improve interpretability, and facilitate empirical model selection.
AI.conclusion: KM-ARL and KM-ADD are practical, theoretically grounded estimators that enable reliable use of QCD criteria in real-world settings with irregular data.

title: KAN-MLP-Mixer: A comprehensive investigation of the usage of Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) for improving IMU-based Human Activity Recognition
summary: Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) have demonstrated an exceptional ability to learn complex functions on clean, low-dimensional data but struggle to maintain performance on noisy and imperfect real-world datasets. In contrast, conventional multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) are far more tolerant to noise and computationally efficient. Replacing all MLP components with KANs in HAR models often degrades accuracy and computation efficiency, highlighting an open challenge: how to combine KANs' precision with MLPs' noise robustness and efficiency. To address this, we systematically explore various placements of KAN modules within deep HAR networks and propose a hybrid architecture that strategically synergizes the strengths of both paradigms, which uses a KAN-based input embedding layer, retains MLP layers for intermediate feature mixing, and introduces a specialized LarctanKAN module for final activity classification. Across eight public HAR datasets, the hybrid KAN-MLP model achieves an average macro F1 score relative improvement of 5.33\% compared pure-MLP model, significantly outperforming standalone KAN and MLP baselines. Furthermore, integrating this hybrid strategy into other state-of-the-art HAR architectures consistently boosts their performance. Our findings demonstrate that a carefully orchestrated combination of KAN, MLP, or other conventional neural components yields more robust and accurate HAR models for real-world wearable sensing environments.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a hybrid KAN-MLP architecture for Human Activity Recognition (HAR) that strategically combines KAN's precision with MLP's noise robustness and efficiency. The hybrid model uses a KAN input embedding layer, MLP intermediate layers, and a specialized LarctanKAN output layer, achieving 5.33% average macro F1 improvement over pure-MLP across eight public HAR datasets.
AI.motivation: KANs excel on clean, low-dimensional data but struggle with noisy real-world datasets; MLPs are noise-tolerant and efficient but less precise. The challenge is how to combine KANs' precision with MLPs' robustness and efficiency for HAR tasks.
AI.method: Systematically explores placements of KAN modules in deep HAR networks, proposing a hybrid architecture with: (1) KAN-based input embedding layer, (2) MLP layers for intermediate feature mixing, (3) specialized LarctanKAN module for final classification.
AI.result: Achieves 5.33% average macro F1 improvement over pure-MLP across eight public HAR datasets, significantly outperforms standalone KAN and MLP baselines, and boosts performance when integrated into other state-of-the-art HAR architectures.
AI.conclusion: A carefully orchestrated hybrid combination of KAN, MLP, or other conventional neural components yields more robust and accurate HAR models for real-world wearable sensing environments.

title: MedFM-Robust: Benchmarking Robustness of Medical Foundation Models
summary: Medical foundation models (MedFMs) have emerged as transformative tools in healthcare, demonstrating capabilities across diverse clinical applications. These models can be broadly categorized into two paradigms: Medical Vision-Language Models (Med-VLMs) and segmentation foundation models. Med-VLMs range from medical-specialized models such as LLaVA-Med and MedGemma, to general-purpose models like GPT-4o and Gemini, all capable of medical image understanding tasks including visual question answering (VQA), report generation, and visual grounding. Concurrently, the Segment Anything Model (SAM) has catalyzed a new generation of medical segmentation models, with adaptations like SAM-Med2D and MedSAM. The widespread clinical deployment of these models thus necessitates rigorous evaluation of their reliability under real-world conditions.

AI.tldr: This paper categorizes medical foundation models into Med-VLMs (e.g., LLaVA-Med, GPT-4o) for image understanding tasks and SAM-based models (e.g., MedSAM) for segmentation, highlighting the need for rigorous reliability evaluation before clinical deployment.
AI.motivation: To establish a taxonomy of emerging medical foundation models and emphasize the critical need for evaluating their reliability under real-world clinical conditions before widespread deployment.
AI.method: The paper provides a conceptual categorization of MedFMs into two paradigms: (1) Medical Vision-Language Models (Med-VLMs) for VQA, report generation, and visual grounding; (2) SAM-based segmentation models adapted for medical imaging.
AI.result: Identifies two major model families (Med-VLMs and segmentation models) and their respective sub-types, noting their diverse clinical capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Rigorous real-world reliability evaluation is necessary before clinical deployment of medical foundation models.

title: Diffusion Graph Posterior Sampling for Nonlinear Inverse Problems with Application to Electrical Impedance Tomography
summary: Deep generative models have emerged as state-of-the-art for solving inverse problems, but applying them to inverse problems for PDEs, like electrical impedance tomography (EIT) remains challenging. Because physical domains are naturally discretized as unstructured meshes rather than regular grids, standard convolutional architectures are often inadequate. In this paper, we propose a novel framework that extends diffusion posterior sampling (DPS) to graph-structured data. We develop an unconditional score-based diffusion model directly on a 2D triangular mesh to learn an accurate prior over the physical solution space. Furthermore, we introduce a regularized variant, RDPS, which incorporates explicit regularization terms, such as total variation and generalized Tikhonov, to complement the implicit diffusion prior and mitigate severe ill-posedness. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real 2D EIT datasets demonstrate that RDPS produces stable, physically plausible reconstructions. Our approach generalizes well to out-of-distribution inclusion geometries, is highly robust to measurement noise, and outperforms current state-of-the-art solvers (e.g., GPnP-BM3D, DP-SGS) in reconstruction accuracy and artifact reduction.

AI.tldr: A framework extending diffusion posterior sampling to graph-structured data (2D triangular meshes) for EIT, combining unconditional score-based diffusion priors with explicit regularization (RDPS) to achieve stable, accurate, and noise-robust reconstructions that outperform existing solvers.
AI.motivation: Applying deep generative models to inverse problems for PDEs (e.g., EIT) is challenging because physical domains use unstructured meshes rather than regular grids, making standard convolutional architectures inadequate.
AI.method: Extends diffusion posterior sampling (DPS) to graph-structured data by developing an unconditional score-based diffusion model on 2D triangular meshes, and introduces RDPS which adds explicit regularization (total variation, generalized Tikhonov) to the implicit diffusion prior.
AI.result: RDPS produces stable, physically plausible reconstructions on synthetic and real 2D EIT data, generalizes well to out-of-distribution geometries, is highly robust to measurement noise, and outperforms GPnP-BM3D and DP-SGS in accuracy and artifact reduction.
AI.conclusion: The proposed graph-based diffusion posterior sampling with regularization effectively addresses ill-posed inverse problems on unstructured meshes, setting a new state-of-the-art for EIT reconstruction.

title: DJSCC-Enabled Multi-User Semantic CSI Feedback for Hybrid Beamforming in Dual-Polarized cmWave Massive MIMO
summary: Driven by the ultra-high throughput requirements of 6G, wireless communications are migrating to centimeter wave (cmWave) bands to overcome the limitations of current spectral resources. Massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) and orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) systems aim to achieve high spectral efficiency in cmWave regimes but are often constrained by the heavy overhead of downlink channel state information (CSI) feedback. This paper proposes a deep learning scheme based on the multi-axis multi-layer perceptron for image processing (MAXIM) architecture for joint semantic CSI feedback and hybrid beamforming in multi-user cmWave MIMO-OFDM systems, which maximizes the downlink sum rate by end-to-end optimization. Specifically, distributed encoders at multiple user equipments (UEs) perform limited CSI feedback, while the decoder at the base station (BS) jointly designs the hybrid beamforming matrices without explicit CSI reconstruction. The uplink transmission is implemented via deep joint source-channel coding (DJSCC) to enhance CSI compression efficiency and noise robustness. Furthermore, considering the high correlation between vertical and horizontal polarization channels in dual-polarized massive MIMO systems, a cross-polarization interaction module is introduced at the UEs to exploit polarization correlations for joint CSI compression. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method improves the downlink sum rate under various signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) conditions with a limited number of feedback symbols, validating its robustness and superiority in multi-user dual-polarized cmWave MIMO-OFDM systems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a deep learning scheme based on the MAXIM architecture for joint semantic CSI feedback and hybrid beamforming in multi-user cmWave MIMO-OFDM systems, achieving improved downlink sum rate with limited feedback symbols.
AI.motivation: Ultra-high throughput requirements of 6G drive migration to cmWave bands, but massive MIMO-OFDM systems face heavy overhead from downlink CSI feedback, limiting spectral efficiency.
AI.method: Uses MAXIM-based deep learning with distributed encoders at UEs for limited CSI feedback and a decoder at the BS for joint hybrid beamforming design. Employs DJSCC for uplink transmission efficiency and a cross-polarization interaction module for dual-polarized massive MIMO systems.
AI.result: The proposed method improves downlink sum rate under various SNR conditions with limited feedback symbols, demonstrating robustness and superiority in multi-user dual-polarized cmWave MIMO-OFDM systems.
AI.conclusion: The MAXIM-based deep learning approach effectively reduces CSI feedback overhead while maintaining high downlink sum rate performance, validated through simulations.

title: ReCrit: Transition-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Scientific Critic Reasoning
summary: Large language models can fail in critic interaction not only by answering incorrectly, but also by abandoning an initially correct scientific solution after user criticism. This is especially risky in scientific reasoning, where user criticism can turn a valid answer into an incorrect one. We frame critic interaction as an inter-turn correctness-transition problem rather than a final-answer accuracy problem, and identify three challenges: transition awareness, decoupling useful correction from harmful sycophancy, and scalable rollout. We propose ReCrit, a transition-aware reinforcement learning framework that decomposes Initial-to-Critic behavior into four quadrants: Correction, Sycophancy, Robustness, and Boundary. ReCrit rewards correction and robustness, penalizes sycophancy, and treats persistent errors as weak boundary signals. To make interaction training practical, ReCrit further uses dynamic asynchronous rollout with tail-adaptive completion to reduce rollout waiting. On three scientific reasoning benchmarks, ChemBench, TRQA, and EarthSE, ReCrit improves average Critic accuracy from 38.15 to 51.49 on Qwen3.5-4B and from 45.40 to 55.59 on Qwen3.5-9B. Ablations show that final-answer rewards provide little interaction-level gain, while transition-aware rewards and quadrant weighting produce more distinguishable training signals and larger net Critic-stage improvement. The code is available at https://github.com/black-yt/ReCrit .

AI.tldr: ReCrit is a transition-aware reinforcement learning framework that improves how LLMs respond to user criticism in scientific reasoning by decomposing behavior into four quadrants (Correction, Sycophancy, Robustness, Boundary) and using dynamic asynchronous rollout. It boosts Critic accuracy from ~38% to ~51% on 4B models and ~45% to ~56% on 9B models across three benchmarks.
AI.motivation: LLMs can fail in critic interaction not just by answering incorrectly, but by abandoning correct solutions after user criticism—a critical problem in scientific reasoning. Prior work focuses on final-answer accuracy, ignoring the inter-turn correctness-transition dynamics.
AI.method: ReCrit is a transition-aware RL framework that: (1) decomposes Initial-to-Critic behavior into four quadrants (Correction, Sycophancy, Robustness, Boundary); (2) rewards correction and robustness, penalizes sycophancy, treats persistent errors as weak boundary signals; (3) uses dynamic asynchronous rollout with tail-adaptive completion to reduce waiting time.
AI.result: On ChemBench, TRQA, and EarthSE, ReCrit improves Critic accuracy from 38.15 to 51.49 (Qwen3.5-4B) and from 45.40 to 55.59 (Qwen3.5-9B). Ablations show transition-aware rewards and quadrant weighting yield more distinguishable signals and larger net improvements than final-answer rewards.
AI.conclusion: ReCrit effectively addresses the critic interaction problem in scientific reasoning by framing it as a correctness-transition problem, and demonstrates that transition-aware RL with quadrant decomposition significantly outperforms final-answer-based approaches.

title: Trustworthy Agent Network: Trust in Agent Networks Must Be Baked In, Not Bolted On
comment: Accepted by SIGKDD 2026 Blue Sky Ideas Track
summary: The rapid advancement of Large Language Models has given rise to autonomous LLM-based agents capable of complex reasoning and execution. As these agents transition from isolated operation to collaborative ecosystems, we witness the emergence of the Agent-to-Agent (A2A) network, a paradigm where heterogeneous agents autonomously coordinate to solve multi-step tasks. While these networks may offer better task performance compared to simply using one agent to complete the entire task, they introduce systemic vulnerabilities, such as adversarial composition, semantic misalignment, and cascading operational failures, that existing agent alignment techniques cannot address. In this vision paper, we argue that the trustworthiness of A2A networks cannot be fully guaranteed via retrofitting on existing protocols that are largely designed for individual agents. Rather, it must be architected from the very beginning of the A2A coordination framework. We present a comprehensive conceptual framework that situates trust in A2A systems through four design pillars.

AI.tldr: This vision paper argues that trust in Agent-to-Agent (A2A) networks must be architected from the ground up, rather than retrofitted, proposing a conceptual framework with four design pillars.
AI.motivation: As LLM-based agents evolve from isolated operation to collaborative A2A networks, new systemic vulnerabilities (adversarial composition, semantic misalignment, cascading failures) emerge that existing agent alignment techniques cannot address.
AI.method: The authors present a conceptual framework that situates trust in A2A systems through four design pillars, advocating for trust to be architected from the beginning of the A2A coordination framework rather than retrofitted.
AI.result: Not explicitly reported; this is a vision paper proposing a framework rather than presenting empirical results.
AI.conclusion: Trustworthiness of A2A networks cannot be fully guaranteed via retrofitting existing protocols; it must be architected from the very beginning of the A2A coordination framework.

title: Personalized Face Privacy Protection From a Single Image
summary: Photos of faces uploaded online are vulnerable to malicious actors who can scrape facial images from online sources and intrude on personal privacy via unauthorized use of facial recognition models. This paper presents FaceCloak, a novel personalized face privacy protection system, which can generate defensive identity-specific universal face privacy masks from a single image of a user, causing facial recognition to fail. FaceCloak introduces a three-stage personalized face perturbation learning methodology: (1) It generates a small set of high-variety synthetic face images of a person based on a single image of the person. (2) It learns face cloaking by adding more protection to key facial-identity leakage regions through iterative perturbation generation over the small set of synthetic images, effectively shifting a user's identity embedding towards a distant anchor identity and away from a similar one. (3) It generates a personalized identity-protective mask in the form of pixel-wise cloaking, which is light-weight and can be efficiently applied to any facial image of a user while maintaining good perceptual quality. Extensive experiments on three popular face datasets across ten recognition models show the effectiveness of FaceCloak compared to 29 other existing representative methods. Code is available at https://github.com/zacharyyahn/FaceCloak

AI.tldr: FaceCloak generates lightweight, personalized universal face privacy masks from a single user photo to defeat facial recognition systems, using synthetic image augmentation and iterative perturbation to shift identity embeddings away from recognition models.
AI.motivation: To protect personal privacy against unauthorized facial recognition by malicious actors who scrape facial images from online sources.
AI.method: Three-stage pipeline: (1) synthetic face image generation from a single photo, (2) iterative perturbation learning to shift identity embeddings toward a distant anchor identity, (3) lightweight pixel-wise cloaking mask generation.
AI.result: Outperforms 29 existing methods across three face datasets and ten recognition models.
AI.conclusion: FaceCloak provides effective, personalized, and efficient face privacy protection with good perceptual quality.

title: Set Shaping Theory as a Complementary Payload-Shaping Layer for Steganography
summary: This paper studies the use of Set Shaping Theory (SST) as a reversible payload-shaping layer for least significant bit (LSB) image steganography. The proposal is not intended to replace existing steganographic methods or to compete with them as a new embedding scheme. Instead, SST is positioned as a complementary preprocessing stage that makes an existing embedding method easier to apply with lower statistical disturbance. The SST transformation increases the message length by K symbols and is implemented with the approximate and fast transformation algorithm developed by Glen Tankersley. Although the embedded payload is lengthened from N to N+K bits, the selected representation can reduce D_KL(P||Q) and therefore make the subsequent steganographic insertion less detectable under histogram-based criteria. Across 1,800 controlled simulations on four synthetic cover-image models, SST reduced D_KL(P||Q) by an average of 25.16 percent relative to a fair N+K LSB baseline, with a 95 percent confidence interval of +/- 1.22 percent. For K=8, the average reduction reached 42.81 percent. Additional robustness simulations with keyed random embedding paths confirmed the effect across several distances: at K=8, SST reduced KL divergence by 42.44 percent, Jensen-Shannon divergence by 29.62 percent, total variation by 12.41 percent, and symmetric chi-square distance by 28.30 percent. An additional image-based matrix-embedding/STC-like simulation showed that SST also reduces the minimum weighted insertion cost: relative to the unshaped K=0 reference, K=8 reduced the cost by 6.93 percent.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using Set Shaping Theory (SST) as a reversible preprocessing layer for LSB image steganography. SST increases the payload length but reduces statistical detectability (KL divergence reduced by up to 42.81%), making subsequent embedding less detectable without replacing existing methods.
AI.motivation: To reduce the statistical detectability of LSB image steganography by adding a reversible preprocessing stage that reshapes the payload before embedding, rather than designing a new embedding scheme.
AI.method: Apply Set Shaping Theory (SST) as a preprocessing layer that transforms the message into a longer representation (N to N+K bits) before LSB embedding. Uses Glen Tankersley's approximate fast transformation algorithm. Evaluates statistical detectability via KL divergence, Jensen-Shannon divergence, total variation, symmetric chi-square distance, and minimum weighted insertion cost.
AI.result: On 1,800 simulations with four synthetic cover models, SST reduced D_KL by 25.16% on average (95% CI ±1.22%). For K=8, average reduction reached 42.81%. Robustness tests showed reductions across all metrics: KL 42.44%, JS 29.62%, TV 12.41%, chi-square 28.30%. In STC-like simulations, K=8 reduced minimum weighted insertion cost by 6.93%.
AI.conclusion: SST is an effective complementary preprocessing layer for LSB steganography that reduces statistical detectability despite increasing payload length. It is not a replacement but an enhancement to existing embedding methods.

title: A Heuristic Approach for Performance Tuning in RL-based Quadrotor Control via Reward Design and Termination Conditions
comment: Accepted in the 34th Mediterranean Conference on Control and Automation
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL)-based quadrotor control policies have achieved impressive performance in tasks such as fast navigation in cluttered environments and drone racing, where the focus is on speed and agility. However, in several applications, such as infrastructure inspection, it is critical to achieve precise, controlled maneuvers with tunable performance. In this article, we present a novel heuristic approach to achieve tunable performance in RL-based Quadrotor control through reward design and termination conditions. We present a novel reward structure containing dual bandwidth exponentials that achieves a baseline critically damped response in setpoint tracking, with low steady-state errors. When trained with a Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm, in conjunction with episode truncation conditions, the desired performance is achieved in 6 million time steps in a sample-efficient manner. In order to tune the performance about the baseline behavior, we present intuitive heuristic rules to adjust the reward weights and exponential coefficients to achieve faster (acrobatic-like) and slower (inspection-like) settling time performance, while retaining the baseline critically damped response and approximately 2\% steady-state error. We evaluate the three RL policies (baseline, acrobatic, and inspection) across 100 trials and show accurate and tunable performance in position and yaw tracking from random initial conditions, thereby demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed heuristic approach.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a heuristic approach using dual-bandwidth exponential rewards and PPO to achieve tunable, critically-damped quadrotor control with low steady-state error, enabling fast (acrobatic) and slow (inspection) performance modes.
AI.motivation: Quadrotor RL policies excel at speed and agility but lack tunable, precise control needed for applications like infrastructure inspection, where controlled maneuvers and adjustable performance are critical.
AI.method: Novel reward structure with dual-bandwidth exponentials for critically-damped setpoint tracking, trained via PPO with episode truncation conditions over 6M time steps. Heuristic rules adjust reward weights and coefficients to tune settling time (acrobatic vs. inspection modes).
AI.result: The three RL policies (baseline, acrobatic, inspection) achieve tunable performance with approximately 2% steady-state error, critically-damped response, and accurate position/yaw tracking across 100 trials.
AI.conclusion: The proposed heuristic reward design enables sample-efficient, tunable quadrotor control with maintained stability, applicable to both agile and precision-demanding tasks.

title: Hyperbolic Frequency Multicarrier Modulation for Wideband Linear Time-Varying Channels
comment: 15 pages, 10 figures, accepted by IEEE TWC
summary: Numerous multicarrier modulation schemes have been proposed recently to enhance the performance in narrowband doubly dispersive channels for emerging high-mobility applications. However, the ultra-reliable modulation framework in wideband linear time-varying (LTV) channels remains an open problem, where the time dilations and contractions brought by the high mobility cannot be ignored for the baseband signal to obtain the constant Doppler shift across the whole transmission band. To solve this problem, we propose the hyperbolic frequency multicarrier (HFMC) waveform in this paper based on the inspiration from affine frequency division multiplexing (AFDM) modulation, where the delay and Doppler shift are absorbed into a 1D shift in the affine domain to provide a compact characterization of doubly dispersive discrete-time channels. By adopting the passband representation of wideband LTV channels and hyperbolic frequency modulated (HFM) signals, we reveal that the Doppler scaling factor brought by the relative mobility can be absorbed into an equivalent delay. The basic principle of HFMC modulation is established by investigating the approximate orthogonality among HFMC subcarriers, which are generated from a basic HFM signal by utilizing uniformly spaced equivalent delay. The spectrum of HFMC subcarriers is also analyzed to evaluate the system capacity, where the overlapping nature in the frequency domain can be observed. The input-output characterization in wideband LTV channels is then executed to confirm the 1D integration of time delay and Doppler scaling factor for each path, which demonstrates the ability to exploit potential multipath diversity. The parameter optimization based on the input-output relation and spectrum analysis is finally developed to balance the efficiency and reliability.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Hyperbolic Frequency Multicarrier (HFMC) modulation for wideband linear time-varying channels, which absorbs delay and Doppler scaling into a 1D shift in the affine domain, enabling reliable communication in high-mobility environments.
AI.motivation: Existing multicarrier schemes address narrowband doubly dispersive channels under constant Doppler shift assumption, but wideband LTV channels experience time dilations/contractions where Doppler scaling varies across the band — this remains an open problem that the paper aims to solve.
AI.method: The authors propose HFMC modulation inspired by AFDM, using hyperbolic frequency modulated (HFM) signals as subcarriers with uniformly spaced equivalent delays. They analyze approximate orthogonality, spectrum characteristics, and derive input-output relationships in wideband LTV channels, followed by parameter optimization.
AI.result: The Doppler scaling factor is absorbed into an equivalent delay, enabling 1D integration of time delay and Doppler scaling per path. The analysis confirms the ability to exploit multipath diversity, and parameter optimization balances efficiency vs. reliability.
AI.conclusion: HFMC is a promising modulation framework for ultra-reliable communication in wideband high-mobility scenarios, offering a compact channel characterization and diversity gains.

title: Theory-optimal Quantization Based on Flatness
summary: Post-training quantization has emerged as a widely adopted technique for compressing and accelerating the inference of Large Language Models (LLMs). The primary challenges in LLMs quantization stem from activation outliers, which significantly degrade model performance especially at lower bit precision. While recent approaches attempt to mitigate outliers through linear transformations across feature dimensions, our analysis reveals that the transformed weights and activations still exhibit persistent outlier patterns with concentrated magnitude distributions. In this paper, we first model the mathematical relationship between quantization error and outliers, and then introduce a new metric Flatness to quantify the distribution of outliers. Based on this, we derive the theoretical optimal solution with respect to Flatness. Building on these insights, we propose Bidirectional Diagonal Quantization (BDQ), a novel post-training quantization framework that effectively disperses outlier patterns through optimized matrix transformations. BDQ strategically distributes outlier magnitudes across matrix dimensions via learned diagonal operations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BDQ establishes a new quantization benchmark. It achieves less than 1\% accuracy drop in W4A4 quantization on the LLaMA-3-8B model. In the more challenging W2A4KV16 experiment, compared to state-of-the-art approaches, BDQ reduces the performance gap by 39.1\% on the DeepSeek-R1-Distill-LLaMA-70B model.

AI.tldr: BDQ is a novel post-training quantization framework that addresses activation outliers in LLMs by using bidirectional diagonal transformations to disperse outlier patterns, achieving state-of-the-art results (e.g., <1% accuracy drop in W4A4 on LLaMA-3-8B).
AI.motivation: The key challenge in LLM quantization is activation outliers, which cause performance degradation at low bit precision. Existing linear transformation methods still leave persistent outlier patterns with concentrated magnitude distributions.
AI.method: The paper first models the mathematical relationship between quantization error and outliers, introduces a metric called Flatness to quantify outlier distribution, then derives the theoretical optimal solution w.r.t. Flatness. Based on this, BDQ uses learned diagonal operations to strategically distribute outlier magnitudes across matrix dimensions via bidirectional diagonal transformations.
AI.result: BDQ achieves <1% accuracy drop with W4A4 quantization on LLaMA-3-8B. In W2A4KV16 on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-LLaMA-70B, BDQ reduces the performance gap by 39.1% compared to state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: BDQ establishes a new quantization benchmark by effectively dispersing outlier patterns through optimized matrix transformations, significantly narrowing the performance gap at extremely low bit precision.

title: Interference-Aware Multi-Task Unlearning
summary: Machine unlearning aims to remove the contribution of designated training data from a trained model while preserving performance on the remaining data. Existing work mainly focuses on single-task settings, whereas modern models often operate in multi-task setups with shared backbones, where removing supervision for one task or instance can unintentionally affect others. We introduce multi-task unlearning with two settings: full-task unlearning, which removes a target instance from all tasks, and partial-task unlearning, which removes supervision only from selected tasks. We show that shared parameters couple the forget and retain sets, causing task-level interference on non-target tasks and instance-level interference on other instances. To address this issue, we propose an interference-aware framework that combines task-aware gradient projection, which constrains updates within task-specific subspaces, with instance-level gradient orthogonalization, which reduces conflicts between forget and retain signals. Experiments on two multi-task computer vision benchmarks across five tasks show that our method achieves effective unlearning while maintaining strong generalization, reducing UIS compared with the strongest baseline by 30.3% in full-task unlearning and 52.9% in partial-task unlearning.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces multi-task unlearning (full-task and partial-task) and proposes an interference-aware framework using task-aware gradient projection and instance-level gradient orthogonalization to reduce task-level and instance-level interference, achieving significant improvements over baselines on multi-task computer vision benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing machine unlearning works focus on single-task settings, but modern models operate in multi-task setups with shared backbones where removing data for one task can unintentionally affect others. The paper aims to address the interference caused by shared parameters between forget and retain sets in multi-task unlearning.
AI.method: The paper proposes an interference-aware framework with two components: (1) task-aware gradient projection that constrains updates within task-specific subspaces, and (2) instance-level gradient orthogonalization that reduces conflicts between forget and retain signals.
AI.result: Experiments on two multi-task computer vision benchmarks across five tasks show the method reduces UIS compared with the strongest baseline by 30.3% in full-task unlearning and 52.9% in partial-task unlearning.
AI.conclusion: The proposed interference-aware framework effectively handles both task-level and instance-level interference in multi-task unlearning, achieving effective unlearning while maintaining strong generalization.

title: LiFT: Lifted Inter-slice Feature Trajectories for 3D Image Generation from 2D Generators
summary: High-resolution 3D medical image generation remains challenging because fully volumetric models are computationally expensive, while efficient 2D slice generators often fail to preserve anatomical consistency across the third dimension. We propose LiFT, a framework for Lifted inter-slice Feature Trajectories that factorizes 3D volume synthesis into per-slice image generation and inter-slice trajectory learning. Rather than modeling the volumetric distribution end-to-end, LiFT treats a volume as an ordered trajectory in feature space, capturing how anatomical structures appear, transform, and disappear across depth. A tri-planar drifting loss aligns the trajectory of generated slices with the trajectories of real volumes, enabling distributional learning over inter-slice progressions in unconditional generation; in paired translation, a bidirectional $z$-context mixer trained against the registered target supplies through-plane coherence while preserving per-slice fidelity. We evaluate LiFT on BraTS 2023 (unconditional and missing-modality MR) and SynthRAD2023 (MR-to-CT). Across these settings, LiFT preserves per-slice quality, approaches the reported cWDM missing-MR reconstruction quality at $\sim$$135\times$ lower inference cost (without formal equivalence testing), and improves through-plane coherence on MR-to-CT relative to a no-mapper ablation, demonstrating that lightweight inter-slice trajectory learning is a viable route to high-resolution 3D medical synthesis.

AI.tldr: LiFT factorizes 3D medical image generation into per-slice generation + lightweight inter-slice trajectory learning, achieving high-resolution results at drastically reduced computational cost compared to full 3D models.
AI.motivation: Full 3D volumetric models are computationally expensive; 2D slice generators lack anatomical consistency across depth. LiFT aims to bridge this gap with a lightweight trajectory learning approach.
AI.method: LiFT treats a 3D volume as an ordered feature trajectory across slices. It uses a tri-planar drifting loss for unconditional generation and a bidirectional z-context mixer for paired translation, learning how structures appear, transform, and disappear across depth.
AI.result: On BraTS 2023 (unconditional & missing-modality MR) and SynthRAD2023 (MR-to-CT), LiFT preserves per-slice quality, achieves comparable cWDM missing-MR reconstruction at ~135× lower inference cost, and improves through-plane coherence over no-mapper ablation.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight inter-slice trajectory learning is a viable and efficient route to high-resolution 3D medical image synthesis, challenging the necessity of full volumetric models.

title: FGSVQA: Frequency-Guided Short-form Video Quality Assessment
summary: Short-form video poses new challenges to the quality assessment of user-generated content (UGC) due to its complex generation pipeline, rapid content variation, and mixed distortions. To address this challenge, we propose an end-to-end video quality assessment (VQA) framework that employs a dense visual encoder based on CLIP, and incorporates compression priors derived from the frequency domain to generate artifact- and structure-aware weight maps for feature aggregation. By explicitly decomposing artifact, structure, and original visual feature branches and adaptively fusing them over time through a learned gating module, the proposed method achieves accurate and efficient quality prediction. Experimental results show that our method achieves strong performance on short-form video datasets in terms of average rank and linear correlation (SRCC: 0.736, PLCC: 0.787), while maintaining efficient inference runtime. The code and additional results are available at: https://github.com/xinyiW915/FGSVQA.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes FGSVQA, an end-to-end VQA framework for short-form video that uses a CLIP-based dense visual encoder, frequency-domain compression priors, and artifact/structure-aware feature aggregation with a learned gating module, achieving strong performance (SRCC: 0.736, PLCC: 0.787).
AI.motivation: Short-form video introduces new challenges for UGC quality assessment due to its complex generation pipeline, rapid content variation, and mixed distortions, which existing methods struggle to handle.
AI.method: A CLIP-based dense visual encoder combined with frequency-domain compression priors to generate artifact- and structure-aware weight maps; explicit decomposition of artifact, structure, and visual feature branches with adaptive temporal fusion via a learned gating module.
AI.result: SRCC of 0.736 and PLCC of 0.787 on short-form video datasets, with efficient inference runtime.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method achieves accurate and efficient quality prediction for short-form video, with publicly available code.

title: Aerial Inspection Behaviors via RL-based Quadrotor Control for Under-canopy Forest Environments
comment: Submitted to 2026 IEEE 22nd International Conference on Automation Science and Engineering
summary: This paper addresses the problem of using a deep Reinforcement Learning (RL)-based low-level Quadrotor controller within an autonomous Quadrotor navigation stack for aerial inspection missions in under-canopy forest environments. Specifically, the article presents an end-to-end (mapping states to RPMs) Quadrotor control policy that achieves inspection view-pose tracking (simultaneous position and yaw reference tracking), which is crucial for various target inspection behaviors and point-to-point navigation in forests. To ensure safe and reliable deployment of the end-to-end RL controller in long-range missions, this article utilizes a higher navigation guidance layer comprising of a Traveling Salesman Problem planner (TSP) and a Rapidly-exploring Random Tree Star (RRT*) planner. Over a known map of a forest and a set of user-specified inspection regions, the TSP planner finds the optimal visitation sequence. Between two target regions, collision-free paths that respect the tracking limitations of the lower end-to-end RL policy are generated by an RRT* planner. Through five target inspection scenarios, this article demonstrates that an RL-based motor-level stabilizing controller, supported by a navigation guidance layer, can be used effectively as the low-level inspection execution module for under-canopy forest inspection missions.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an end-to-end deep RL-based quadrotor controller for under-canopy forest inspection, combined with a TSP and RRT* planning layer for safe long-range navigation and view-pose tracking.
AI.motivation: To enable autonomous quadrotor inspection in under-canopy forest environments where traditional controllers struggle, by using deep RL for low-level control.
AI.method: An end-to-end RL policy maps states directly to RPMs for simultaneous position and yaw tracking, supported by a higher navigation layer with TSP for optimal visitation ordering and RRT* for collision-free path planning respecting RL policy limits.
AI.result: Demonstrated effective inspection view-pose tracking and point-to-point navigation across five target inspection scenarios in forest environments.
AI.conclusion: RL-based motor-level stabilizing control, augmented with a navigation guidance layer, can serve as a reliable low-level inspection module for under-canopy forest missions.

title: PROWL: Prioritized Regret-Driven Optimization for World Model Learning
summary: Modern action-conditioned video world models achieve strong short-horizon visual realism, yet remain unreliable on rare, interaction-critical transitions that dominate downstream planning and policy performance. Because passive demonstration data systematically under-samples these high-impact regimes, improving robustness requires actively eliciting model failures rather than relying on their natural occurrence. We introduce a KL-constrained adversarial curriculum in which a policy is trained to expose high-error trajectories of a diffusion-based world model while remaining close to the behavior distribution. The world model is continuously fine-tuned on these adversarially discovered trajectories, yielding an adversarial training loop that converts rare failures into a stable, near-distribution training signal without drifting into out-of-distribution exploitation. To maintain pressure on unresolved weaknesses as the model improves, we propose a Prioritized Adversarial Trajectory (PAT) buffer that re-ranks trajectories based on prediction error, action fidelity, and learning progress, focusing training on unresolved failure modes rather than repeatedly revisiting solved cases. We implement our approach in the MineRL framework and evaluate it on held-out out-of-distribution trajectories; PROWL improves robustness over models trained on passive data alone, reveals reward-hacking behaviors under weak behavioral constraints, and demonstrates that effective adversarial world-model training critically depends on balancing exploratory failure discovery with explicit behavioral regularization. Our results suggest that scalable world models benefit not only from larger datasets, but also from selectively generating informative training data.

AI.tldr: PROWL uses a KL-constrained adversarial curriculum with a Prioritized Adversarial Trajectory buffer to actively discover and train on high-error trajectories of diffusion-based world models, improving robustness on rare but critical transitions.
AI.motivation: Passive demonstration data systematically under-samples rare, interaction-critical transitions that dominate downstream planning/policy performance; world models remain unreliable on these high-impact regimes.
AI.method: (1) KL-constrained adversarial curriculum: trains a policy to expose high-error trajectories of a diffusion world model while staying near the behavior distribution. (2) Prioritized Adversarial Trajectory (PAT) buffer: re-ranks trajectories by prediction error, action fidelity, and learning progress. (3) Adversarial training loop: continuously fine-tunes the world model on discovered trajectories.
AI.result: PROWL improves robustness over passive-data-trained models on held-out OOD trajectories in MineRL; reveals reward-hacking behaviors under weak behavioral constraints; effective adversarial training requires balancing failure discovery with behavioral regularization.
AI.conclusion: Scalable world models benefit not only from larger datasets, but also from selectively generating informative training data via adversarial curriculum learning.

title: Embedding by Elicitation: Dynamic Representations for Bayesian Optimization of System Prompts
summary: System prompts are a central control mechanism in modern AI systems, shaping behavior across conversations, tasks, and user populations. Yet they are difficult to tune when feedback is available only as aggregate metrics rather than per-example labels, failures, or critiques. We study this aggregate feedback setting as sample-constrained black-box optimization over discrete, variable-length text. We introduce ReElicit, a Bayesian optimization framework based on \emph{embedding by elicitation}. Given a task description, previously evaluated prompts, and scalar scores, an LLM elicits a compact, interpretable feature space and maps prompts into it. Leveraging a probabilistic Gaussian process surrogate, an acquisition function then selects target feature vectors, which the LLM realizes and refines into deployable system prompts. Re-eliciting the feature space as new evaluations arrive lets the representation adapt to the observed prompt-score history. We evaluate the setting using offline benchmark accuracy as a controlled aggregate proxy: the optimizer observes one scalar score per prompt and no per-example labels, errors, or critiques. Across ten system prompt optimization tasks with a 30 total evaluation budget, ReElicit achieves the strongest aggregate performance profile among representative aggregate-only prompt-optimization baselines. These results suggest that LLMs can serve as adaptive semantic representation builders, not only prompt generators, for Bayesian optimization over natural-language artifacts.

AI.tldr: ReElicit is a Bayesian optimization framework that uses LLMs to elicit compact, interpretable feature spaces from previously evaluated prompts and scores, enabling efficient system prompt optimization under a tight aggregate feedback budget (30 evaluations), outperforming other aggregate-only baselines across ten prompt optimization tasks.
AI.motivation: System prompts are difficult to tune when feedback is available only as aggregate metrics (e.g., one scalar score per prompt) rather than per-example labels, failures, or critiques.
AI.method: ReElicit: An LLM elicits a compact, interpretable feature space from task descriptions, past prompts, and scalar scores; maps prompts into this space; a Gaussian process surrogate models prompt-score relationships; an acquisition function selects target feature vectors; the LLM realizes these into deployable prompts. The feature space is re-elicited adaptively as new evaluations arrive.
AI.result: Across ten system prompt optimization tasks with a 30 total evaluation budget, ReElicit achieves the strongest aggregate performance profile among representative aggregate-only prompt-optimization baselines.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can serve as adaptive semantic representation builders (not just prompt generators) for Bayesian optimization over natural-language artifacts.

title: Learning Long-Term Temporal Dependencies in Photovoltaic Power Output Prediction Through Multi-Horizon Forecasting
summary: The rapid global expansion of solar photovoltaic (PV) capacity-reaching a record 597 GW in 2024-highlights the urgent need for robust forecasting models to mitigate the grid instability caused by the intermittent nature of solar irradiance. While deep learning-based direct forecasting using ground-based sky images (GSI) has emerged as a dominant approach, existing literature is often constrained by single-architecture evaluations and an exclusive focus on single-horizon (point) prediction. This paper proposes a transition from traditional single-horizon estimation toward a multi-horizon forecasting framework, leading to an architecture-independent improvement in accuracy. We hypothesize and demonstrate experimentally that joint optimization over a sequence of future values allows deep neural networks to better capture latent inter-step temporal dependencies by avoiding precocious convergence of the network in terms of both weight gradients and filter diversity. Leveraging this architecture-independent improvement that integrates sequential sky imagery with historical PV generation data, we evaluate the models' abilities to predict power output across multiple discrete future time steps simultaneously. Our methodology is validated through a comparative analysis across diverse deep learning architectures. The results demonstrate that this multi-horizon approach significantly enhances predictive accuracy and robustness across the entire forecast horizon while maintaining computational parsimony. By achieving superior performance with negligible overhead compared to single-horizon models, this work provides a scalable and efficient solution to improve the resilience of modern power grids.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a multi-horizon forecasting framework for solar PV power output using ground-based sky images and historical data, demonstrating architecture-independent accuracy improvements over traditional single-horizon models through joint optimization across multiple future time steps.
AI.motivation: The rapid expansion of solar PV capacity (597 GW in 2024) creates urgent need for better forecasting to mitigate grid instability from solar irradiance intermittency, while existing deep learning approaches are limited by single-architecture evaluations and single-horizon (point) prediction focus.
AI.method: Transition from single-horizon to multi-horizon forecasting by jointly optimizing over a sequence of future values, integrating sequential sky imagery with historical PV generation data, and evaluating across diverse deep learning architectures to show architecture-independent improvement.
AI.result: Multi-horizon approach significantly enhances predictive accuracy and robustness across the entire forecast horizon while maintaining computational parsimony, with negligible overhead compared to single-horizon models.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a scalable and efficient solution to improve power grid resilience by demonstrating that joint optimization over future sequences avoids precocious network convergence and better captures latent inter-step temporal dependencies.

title: DAFT-s-AFDM Enabled ISAC Systems: Ambiguity Function Analysis and Waveform Design
comment: 13 pages, 10 figures, submitted to IEEE JSAC
summary: Discrete affine Fourier transform spread affine frequency division multiplexing (DAFT-s-AFDM) is a promising waveform for integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) due to its low peak-to-average power ratio, robustness to Doppler shifts, and reduced multiuser interference in the uplink transmission. This paper presents a comprehensive ambiguity function (AF) analysis of DAFT-s-AFDM and derives the closed-form expression for the AF magnitude expectation. Several key insights into the impact of DAFT-s-AFDM parameters on ISAC performance are revealed, thus providing concrete guidance for the subsequent waveform design. Building on these insights, a novel probabilistic constellation shaping (PCS) framework is proposed for ISAC waveform enhancement, where the communication throughput and the sensing AF characteristics are jointly optimized by addressing a multi-objective problem. An efficient algorithm based on a closed-form bit error rate expression is developed to obtain the Pareto-optimal solutions. Extensive simulations validate the theoretical results and that the proposed PCS-enhanced DAFT-s-AFDM can significantly outperform the classical counterparts, achieving a superior and highly controllable tradeoff between the dual-functional performances.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the ambiguity function of DAFT-s-AFDM for ISAC, derives closed-form expressions, and proposes a probabilistic constellation shaping framework to jointly optimize communication throughput and sensing performance, achieving superior tradeoffs.
AI.motivation: To address the need for a waveform that simultaneously achieves low PAPR, Doppler robustness, and reduced multiuser interference in ISAC uplink, while providing concrete guidance for waveform design based on AF analysis.
AI.method: Derives closed-form expression for AF magnitude expectation of DAFT-s-AFDM; proposes a PCS framework that formulates a multi-objective optimization problem balancing communication throughput and sensing AF characteristics, solved via an efficient algorithm using closed-form BER expression.
AI.result: Theoretical AF analysis validated by simulations; proposed PCS-enhanced DAFT-s-AFDM significantly outperforms classical counterparts, achieving superior and highly controllable ISAC performance tradeoffs.
AI.conclusion: DAFT-s-AFDM with PCS optimization is a highly effective ISAC waveform, offering controllable tradeoffs between communication and sensing functions.

title: Adaptive Multi-Scale Goodness Aggregation for Forward-Forward Learning
summary: We propose Adaptive Multi-Scale Goodness Aggregation (AMSGA), a novel extension of the Forward-Forward (FF) algorithm designed to improve stability, robustness, and generalization in local-learning neural networks. AMSGA addresses several limitations of the original FF framework by introducing multi-scale goodness aggregation across local, intermediate, and global representations; adaptive curriculum-guided hard negative mining; layer-dependent adaptive thresholds; and a warm-up cosine annealing learning-rate schedule for improved optimization stability. Together, these modifications strengthen the FF paradigm while preserving its biologically plausible and memory-efficient properties. Experiments on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST demonstrate consistent performance improvements over the baseline FF algorithm, achieving up to +1.45% improvement on MNIST and +1.50% improvement on Fashion-MNIST without significant computational overhead. Our results suggest that local learning methods can become substantially more competitive when goodness estimation and training dynamics are carefully designed.

AI.tldr: AMSGA enhances the Forward-Forward algorithm with multi-scale goodness aggregation, adaptive negative mining, layer-dependent thresholds, and cosine annealing schedule, achieving ~1.5% accuracy gains on MNIST/Fashion-MNIST while maintaining biological plausibility and memory efficiency.
AI.motivation: To address limitations of the original Forward-Forward algorithm in stability, robustness, and generalization by designing a better goodness estimation and training dynamics framework.
AI.method: Introduces four key extensions: (1) multi-scale goodness aggregation across local, intermediate, and global layers; (2) adaptive curriculum-guided hard negative mining; (3) layer-dependent adaptive thresholds; (4) warm-up cosine annealing learning-rate schedule.
AI.result: +1.45% improvement on MNIST and +1.50% on Fashion-MNIST over baseline FF algorithm, with no significant computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: Local learning methods can become substantially more competitive when goodness estimation and training dynamics are carefully designed; AMSGA preserves biologically plausible and memory-efficient properties.

title: DecisionBench: A Benchmark for Emergent Delegation in Long-Horizon Agentic Workflows
comment: 28 pages, 9 figures, 11 tables. Code and data: https://huggingface.co/decisionbench
summary: We introduce DecisionBench, a benchmark substrate for emergent delegation in long-horizon agentic workflows. The substrate fixes a task suite (GAIA, tau-bench, BFCL multi-turn), a peer-model pool (11 models, 7 vendor families), a delegation interface (call_model plus an optional read_profile channel), a deterministic skill-annotation layer, and a multi-axis metric suite covering quality, cost, latency, delegation rate, routing fidelity-at-k, vendor self-preference, and a counterfactual-delegation ceiling. The substrate is agnostic to how peer information is generated or delivered, so learned routers, richer peer memories, adaptive profile construction, and multi-step delegation can all be evaluated against it. We characterize the substrate with a five-condition reference sweep on the full pool (n=23,375 task instances). Three benchmark-level findings emerge: (i) mean end-task quality is statistically indistinguishable across the four awareness conditions (|beta| <= 0.010, p >= 0.21), so quality-only evaluation would miss the orchestration signal; (ii) routing fidelity-at-1 ranges from 7.5% to 29.5% across conditions at near-equal mean quality, with delivery channel (on-demand tool vs. preloaded description) dominating description content; (iii) a counterfactual ceiling places perfect delegation 15-31 percentage points above measured performance on every suite, locating large unrealized headroom for future orchestration methods. We release the substrate, annotation layer, reference intervention suite, analysis pipeline, and 220 per-condition run archives.

AI.tldr: DecisionBench is a standardized benchmark for evaluating delegation in long-horizon AI agent workflows, revealing that quality metrics alone miss orchestration signals, routing fidelity varies by delivery channel, and there's 15-31% headroom for improvement.
AI.motivation: To provide a standardized, multi-metric evaluation substrate for emergent delegation in agentic workflows, addressing the gap where quality-only evaluation fails to capture orchestration performance.
AI.method: Fixed task suite (GAIA, tau-bench, BFCL multi-turn), 11-model peer pool, delegation interface with call_model and read_profile, deterministic skill annotation, and multi-axis metrics (quality, cost, latency, delegation rate, routing fidelity-at-k, vendor self-preference, counterfactual ceiling).
AI.result: (i) Mean quality indistinguishable across awareness conditions; (ii) routing fidelity-at-1 ranges 7.5%-29.5% with delivery channel dominating content; (iii) counterfactual ceiling shows 15-31% headroom above measured performance.
AI.conclusion: DecisionBench reveals substantial untapped potential in delegation orchestration and provides a reusable substrate for future methods.

title: CRAFT: Critic-Refined Adaptive Key-Frame Targeting for Multimodal Video Question Answering
comment: Accepted at ACL 2026 Multimodal Augmented Generation via MultimodAl Retrieval Workshop
summary: Grounded multi-video question answering over real-world news events requires systems to surface query-relevant evidence across heterogeneous video archives while attributing every claim to its supporting source. We introduce CRAFT (Critic-Refined Adaptive Key-Frame Targeting), a query-conditioned pipeline that combines dynamic keyframe selection, per-video ASR with multilingual fallback, and a hybrid critic loop to iteratively verify and repair claims before consolidation. The pipeline integrates UNLI temporal entailment, DeBERTa-v3 cross-claim screening, and a Llama-3.2-3B adjudicator, with a final citation-merging stage that emits each fact once with all supporting source identifiers. On MAGMaR 2026, CRAFT achieves the best overall average (0.739), reference recall (0.810), and citation F1 (0.635). We further evaluate on a MAGMaR-style conversion of WikiVideo with 52 non-overlapping event queries, where CRAFT also performs strongly (0.823 Avg), showing that its claim-centric evidence aggregation generalizes beyond MAGMaR. Ablations show that atomic claims, ASR, and the critic loop drive the main gains over the vanilla query-conditioned baseline. Code and implementation details are publicly available at https://github.com/bhosalems/CRAFT.

AI.tldr: CRAFT is a query-conditioned pipeline for grounded multi-video QA that combines dynamic keyframe selection, multilingual ASR, and a hybrid critic loop for iterative claim verification and citation, achieving state-of-the-art results on MAGMaR 2026 and strong performance on WikiVideo.
AI.motivation: To enable accurate question answering over real-world news events across heterogeneous video archives with proper source attribution for every claim.
AI.method: CRAFT pipeline: dynamic keyframe selection, per-video ASR with multilingual fallback, hybrid critic loop (UNLI temporal entailment, DeBERTa-v3 cross-claim screening, Llama-3.2-3B adjudicator), and citation-merging stage.
AI.result: Best overall average (0.739), reference recall (0.810), and citation F1 (0.635) on MAGMaR 2026; strong performance on WikiVideo (0.823 Avg); ablations show atomic claims, ASR, and critic loop drive main gains.
AI.conclusion: CRAFT's claim-centric evidence aggregation generalizes beyond MAGMaR, and iterative verification is key to performance improvement.

title: On the exact decoding error probability exponent of the random coding on BSC
summary: For the information transmission over a binary symmetric channel the random coding is used. The transmission of exponential number of messages is considered. The exact decoding error probability exponent is derived. The proof is based on the new results on the distribution of a certain sum of random variables.

AI.tldr: This paper derives the exact decoding error probability exponent for random coding over a binary symmetric channel, using new results on the distribution of sums of random variables.
AI.motivation: To establish the exact error exponent for random coding over a binary symmetric channel when transmitting an exponential number of messages.
AI.method: The proof relies on new findings about the distribution of a specific sum of random variables to analyze decoding error probability.
AI.result: The exact decoding error probability exponent is obtained for random coding over a binary symmetric channel.
AI.conclusion: The exact error exponent is derived, advancing the understanding of channel coding performance limits.

title: Graph Neural Planning and Predictive Control for Multi-Robot Communication-Constrained Unlabeled Motion Planning
comment: 8 pages, 6 figures, Accepted at the IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA) 2026
summary: The multi-robot unlabeled motion planning problem of concurrently assigning robots to goals and generating safe trajectories is central in many collaborative tasks. Recent Graph Neural Network methods offer scalable decentralized solutions but rely on simplified dynamics and simulation environments, overlooking key challenges of real-world deployment such as dynamic feasibility and communication constraints. To address these gaps, we propose a hierarchical framework that combines a Graph ATtention Planner (GATP) with a decentralized Nonlinear Model Predictive Controller (NMPC). GATP provides intermediate subgoals through multi-robot cooperation, and the NMPC enforces safety under nonlinear dynamics and actuation constraints. We evaluate our framework in both simulation and real-world quadrotor experiments. Thanks to attention mechanisms and minimal communication requirements, we demonstrate improved generalization to larger teams, robustness to communication delays up to 200 ms and practical feasibility with decentralized on-board inference.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a hierarchical framework combining a Graph ATtention Planner (GATP) with a decentralized Nonlinear Model Predictive Controller (NMPC) for multi-robot unlabeled motion planning, achieving robust performance in simulation and real-world quadrotor experiments under communication delays and dynamic constraints.
AI.motivation: Existing Graph Neural Network methods for multi-robot motion planning overlook real-world challenges such as dynamic feasibility and communication constraints, relying on simplified dynamics and simulation environments.
AI.method: A hierarchical framework with GATP for generating intermediate subgoals via multi-robot cooperation and decentralized NMPC for enforcing safety under nonlinear dynamics and actuation constraints.
AI.result: Improved generalization to larger teams, robustness to communication delays up to 200 ms, and practical feasibility demonstrated through both simulation and real-world quadrotor experiments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework bridges the gap between scalable GNN-based planning and real-world deployment by addressing dynamic feasibility and communication constraints, validated on real quadrotors.

title: Practical RIS Gain without the Pain via Randomization and Opportunistic Scheduling in 5G~NR Wireless Systems: Theory and Experiments
comment: Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Communications
summary: In this paper, we theoretically analyze and experimentally demonstrate the performance gains achievable by integrating an in-house built reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) with a 5G new radio (NR) system implemented using the OpenAirInterface (OAI) software stack. Unlike conventional RIS-assisted systems that rely on explicit channel state information (CSI) estimation followed by RIS phase configuration optimization, we adopt a low-complexity approach in which the RIS phase states are randomly switched among predefined configurations. The resulting channel fluctuations are opportunistically exploited by the inherent proportional fair (PF) scheduling mechanism of 5G NR. We develop a theoretical framework that characterizes the interaction between RIS switching dynamics and PF scheduling. Based on this framework and the associated analysis, we provide design guidelines for selecting the RIS switching time $T_s$ and the PF throughput averaging window $T_c$ that maximize the system throughput. Experimental evaluations on the 5G NR testbed demonstrate improvements in key performance metrics, including reference signal received power (RSRP), block error rate (BLER), modulation and coding scheme (MCS) index, and throughput. Our key takeaway is that randomly configured RIS operation with appropriately chosen system parameters can achieve performance comparable to optimized RIS designs, with no additional overhead compared to a conventional 5G NR system. More importantly, it requires no coordination between the RIS and the 5G NR system.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that a randomly-switched RIS can enhance 5G NR performance by leveraging the existing PF scheduler, achieving gains comparable to optimized RIS designs without requiring coordination or explicit CSI estimation.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate that a low-complexity, random-switching RIS can improve 5G NR system performance without the overhead of explicit CSI estimation and phase optimization, and without requiring coordination between RIS and the 5G network.
AI.method: Integrate an in-house RIS with an OAI-based 5G NR system. Use random switching among predefined RIS phase configurations. Exploit the inherent PF scheduling mechanism of 5G NR to opportunistically utilize resulting channel fluctuations. Develop a theoretical framework to analyze RIS switching dynamics and PF scheduling interaction, providing design guidelines for optimal T_s and T_c selection.
AI.result: Experimental improvements in RSRP, BLER, MCS index, and throughput. Performance comparable to optimized RIS designs with no additional overhead and no coordination between RIS and the 5G NR system.
AI.conclusion: Randomly configured RIS with appropriately chosen switching time and averaging window can achieve performance gains similar to optimized RIS, while avoiding coordination overhead and complexity.

title: Block-Based Double Decoders
summary: Encoder-decoder models offer substantial inference-time savings over decoder-only models, but their pretraining objectives suffer from sparse supervision and dynamic sequence lengths, keeping them out of practice at scale. We propose block-based double decoders, a novel transformer architecture that utilizes doubly-causal block-based attention masks to train with full loss supervision and static sequence packing, combining decoder-only training efficiency with encoder-decoder inference efficiency. In scaling law experiments, block-based double decoders strongly outperform encoder-decoders and closely track decoder-only models across scales. At inference time, they cut KV-cache memory and per-token compute by at least 2/3 without sacrificing prefill caching or other existing inference optimizations available to decoder-only models.

AI.tldr: Block-based double decoders combine decoder-only training efficiency with encoder-decoder inference efficiency, outperforming encoder-decoders in scaling laws while reducing KV-cache memory by 2/3 at inference time.
AI.motivation: Encoder-decoder models suffer from sparse supervision and dynamic sequence lengths, preventing widespread adoption at scale despite their inference-time savings over decoder-only models.
AI.method: Proposes a novel transformer architecture with doubly-causal block-based attention masks that enable full loss supervision training and static sequence packing.
AI.result: In scaling law experiments, the method outperforms encoder-decoders and closely tracks decoder-only models; at inference, it cuts KV-cache memory and per-token compute by ≥2/3 without sacrificing existing optimizations.
AI.conclusion: Block-based double decoders effectively bridge the gap between decoder-only training efficiency and encoder-decoder inference efficiency, making them a practical alternative for large-scale deployment.

title: POLAR-Bench: A Diagnostic Benchmark for Privacy-Utility Trade-offs in LLM Agents
summary: LLM agents increasingly have access to private user data and act on the user's behalf when interacting with third-party systems. The user defines what may and must not be shared, and the agent must robustly follow that intent even when third-party systems behave adversarially. We introduce POLAR-Bench (Policy-aware adversarial Benchmark), in which a trusted model with a privacy policy and a task converses with a third-party model that adversarially probes for both task-relevant and protected attributes. Across 10 domains and 7,852 samples, we score privacy and utility by deterministic set-membership and vary privacy policy dimension and attack strategy along two orthogonal axes, producing a 5 times 5 diagnostic surface per model. Our results reveal a sharp split: current frontier models withhold over 99% of protected attributes, while smaller open-weight models in the 1--30B range, the class users most commonly run as their own trusted agent on-device or via private inference, score notably worse, with the weakest leaking over half. POLAR-Bench thus localizes where each model's intent-following breaks down, providing a foothold for privacy alignment where it matters most.

AI.tldr: POLAR-Bench evaluates LLM agents' ability to protect private user data when interacting with adversarial third-party systems, finding that frontier models withhold >99% of protected attributes but smaller open-weight models (1-30B range) leak over half.
AI.motivation: LLM agents access private user data and interact with third-party systems that may behave adversarially, yet there is no systematic benchmark to evaluate whether agents robustly follow user-defined privacy policies under such adversarial probing.
AI.method: The authors introduce POLAR-Bench, where a trusted agent with a privacy policy converses with an adversarial third-party model. They use 10 domains and 7,852 samples, scoring privacy and utility via deterministic set-membership. Privacy policy dimension and attack strategy are varied along two orthogonal axes, producing a 5×5 diagnostic surface per model.
AI.result: Frontier models withhold over 99% of protected attributes, while smaller open-weight models (1-30B range) score notably worse, with the weakest leaking over half of protected attributes.
AI.conclusion: POLAR-Bench localizes where each model's intent-following breaks down, providing a diagnostic foothold for improving privacy alignment, especially for smaller models that users commonly run as trusted on-device agents.

title: Probability-Conserving Flow Guidance
summary: Diffusion and flow-based generative models dominate visual synthesis, with guidance aligning samples to user input and improving perceptual quality. However, Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) and extrapolation-based methods are heuristic linear combinations of velocities/scores that ignore the generative manifold geometry, breaking probability conservation and driving samples off the learned manifold under strong guidance. We analyse guidance through the continuity equation and show its effect decomposes into a divergence term and a score-parallel term defined invariantly across parameterisations. We prove the divergence term blows up structurally as sampling approaches the data manifold, motivating a time-dependent schedule alongside score-parallel attenuation. The resulting plug-and-play rule, Adaptive Manifold Guidance (AdaMaG), bounds both terms at no additional inference cost. Finally, we show that most empirical heuristics for reducing saturation or improving generation quality correspond directly to the two terms in our decomposition. Across image generation benchmarks, AdaMaG improves realism, reduces hallucinations, and induces controlled desaturation in high-guidance regimes.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Adaptive Manifold Guidance (AdaMaG), a principled guidance method for diffusion and flow-based generative models that respects the generative manifold geometry by decomposing guidance effects via the continuity equation, bounding divergence and score-parallel terms to prevent samples from drifting off the manifold under strong guidance.
AI.motivation: Existing guidance methods like Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) are heuristic linear combinations that ignore the geometric structure of the generative manifold, breaking probability conservation and pushing samples off the learned manifold under strong guidance.
AI.method: The authors analyze guidance through the continuity equation, decomposing its effect into a divergence term and a score-parallel term. They prove the divergence term blows up as sampling approaches the data manifold, motivating a time-dependent schedule with score-parallel attenuation. The resulting rule, Adaptive Manifold Guidance (AdaMaG), bounds both terms at no additional inference cost.
AI.result: AdaMaG improves realism, reduces hallucinations, and induces controlled desaturation in high-guidance regimes across image generation benchmarks. Most empirical heuristics for reducing saturation or improving quality correspond to the two terms in the decomposition.
AI.conclusion: The proposed AdaMaG provides a principled, geometrically grounded alternative to heuristic CFG-based guidance, offering better performance at no extra inference cost.

title: Bilateral Teleoperation with Compliant 6-DOF Pose-and-Force Sensing
summary: Existing bilateral teleoperation platforms still rely on costly rigid six-axis force/torque sensors, tightly coupled leader-follower hardware, and kilohertz control loops. We present a Cartesian bilateral framework built on the hardware-agnostic WinGs Operating Studio (WOS) middleware, in which a low-cost compliant 6-DOF pose-and-force sensing end-effector, Delta6, is mounted on both sides so that each manipulator behaves as an end-effector 6-DOF series elastic actuator (SEA). The leader runs a damping-only admittance loop with a 6-D biquad notch filter; the follower realizes a stiffness-damping impedance through a position-based outer loop with a PID wrench-to-pose mapping. Three time scales (hardware I/O, mid-rate impedance/admittance, low-rate teleoperation messages) are explicitly decoupled, enabling the same application to drive heterogeneous arms. On a Lite6/FR3 testbed at 150 Hz, the system tracks stably under delays up to $120\pm40$ ms and 1% packet loss, matches the prescribed virtual stiffness in contact, and shows a favorable cumulative energy signature in passivity-style tests.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a Cartesian bilateral teleoperation framework using low-cost compliant end-effectors and a middleware (WOS) to decouple hardware, impedance/admittance control, and teleoperation layers, achieving stable operation at 150 Hz with up to 120ms delay and 1% packet loss.
AI.motivation: To replace costly rigid six-axis force/torque sensors and tightly coupled leader-follower hardware with a low-cost, hardware-agnostic bilateral teleoperation system.
AI.method: A Delta6 compliant 6-DOF pose-and-force sensing end-effector mounted on both sides, with damping-only admittance control on the leader, stiffness-damping impedance on the follower, and three time-scale decoupling via WOS middleware.
AI.result: Achieves stable tracking at 150 Hz under up to 120±40 ms delay and 1% packet loss, matches prescribed virtual stiffness, and shows favorable passivity-style energy behavior.
AI.conclusion: The proposed low-cost, middleware-based bilateral teleoperation framework can effectively replace traditional rigid sensor-based systems with stable performance under realistic network conditions.

title: Compositional Literary Primitives in Instruction-Tuned LLMs: Cross-Architectural SAE Features for Self, Style, and Affect
summary: We characterize a compositional architecture of literary primitives in two instruction-tuned large language models (Llama 3.1 8B-Instruct and Gemma 2 9B-IT) via sparse autoencoders on mid-depth residual streams. Four feature classes emerge: naming-gates that promote lexical tokens of a target affect, an eleven-self cluster of first-person register features, stylistic register modulators (show-don't-tell and defamiliarization), and compositional emotions that arise only from multi-feature steering. Under a forced-choice 5-LLM judge panel applied to a 27-category emotion taxonomy (Cowen-Keltner), Llama reaches full 27/27 coverage by combining naming-gates, multi-feature recipes, and single self-feature steering; Gemma reaches 23/27 with adoration as the single residual strict-fail. Under random judging, the per-cell pass probability is on the order of $10^{-3}$ and the expected number of two-seed false-positive cells across the catalog is negligible, so the observed coverage is not consistent with chance. A cross-architectural asymmetry sits in the strict-versus-soft judge contrast: on the same generations, judges agree more often on Llama outputs than on Gemma outputs because Llama outputs name the target affect more directly while Gemma outputs evoke it through scene and imagery.
