AI.result: Classical RNNs and Echo State Networks perform well on basic retention under strict parameter budgets. However, only attention mechanisms (Transformers) and modern state-space models (Mamba) maintain high performance as reasoning complexity and task difficulty increase.
AI.conclusion: CogScale provides an effective lightweight evaluation framework for sequential architectures. The results highlight a trade-off: simpler models work for basic tasks under tight budgets, but more sophisticated architectures are necessary for complex reasoning at scale.

title: Thinking in Scales: Accelerating Gigapixel Pathology Image Analysis via Adaptive Continuous Reasoning
summary: Traditional whole slide image (WSI) analysis methods typically rely on the multiple instance learning (MIL) paradigm, which extracts patch-level features at high magnification and aggregates them for slide-level prediction. However, such exhaustive patch-level processing is computationally expensive, severely limiting the efficiency and scalability of WSI analysis. To address this challenge, we propose PathCTM (a Pathology-oriented Continuous Thought Model) that enables token-efficient scale-space continuous reasoning for gigapixel WSIs. PathCTM formulates diagnostic inference as a dynamic sequential information pursuit. It progressively transitions from low-magnification global to high-magnification local inspection, and adaptively terminates inference when sufficient evidence is gathered to effectively bound decision uncertainty. Specifically, it uses conditional computation for dynamic scale switching with attention-guided region pruning, coupled with confidence-aware early stopping. Extensive experiments demonstrate that, compared with standard MIL-based methods, PathCTM reduces the number of required image patches by 95.95% and shortens inference time by approximately 95.62%, while maintaining AUC without degradation. Code is available at https://github.com/JSGe-AI/PathCTM.

AI.tldr: PathCTM is a token-efficient model for gigapixel WSI analysis that uses dynamic scale-space reasoning, progressively moving from low to high magnification with adaptive early stopping, achieving ~96% reduction in patches and inference time without sacrificing AUC.
AI.motivation: Traditional MIL-based WSI methods process all patches at high magnification exhaustively, leading to massive computational costs and poor scalability.
AI.method: PathCTM formulates inference as dynamic sequential information pursuit with conditional computation for scale switching, attention-guided region pruning, and confidence-aware early stopping.
AI.result: Reduces required patches by 95.95% and inference time by 95.62% while maintaining AUC compared to standard MIL methods.
AI.conclusion: PathCTM achieves dramatic efficiency gains for WSI analysis without accuracy degradation, offering a scalable alternative to exhaustive patch-level MIL.

title: CADENet: Condition-Adaptive Asynchronous Dual-Stream Enhancement Network for Adverse Weather Perception in Autonomous Driving
summary: Adverse weather (rain, fog, sand, and snow) degrades camera-based object detection in autonomous vehicles. Existing enhancement-then-detect approaches stall the safety-critical perception loop, violating hard real-time requirements. Progress on this problem is also constrained by an under-recognized evaluation ceiling: ground truth annotated on degraded images cannot credit a detector that recovers objects the annotators themselves could not see, so a genuinely useful enhancement can register as a near-flat F1 gain. This paper presents CADENet (Condition-Adaptive Asynchronous Dual-stream Enhancement Network), a training-free three-thread system: Thread S (YOLOv11n) delivers detections at full frame rate with zero added latency; Thread Q applies condition-adaptive enhancement (CAPE) and fuses results via entropy-guided NMS (EG-NMS) without blocking Thread S; Thread E provides CLIP zero-shot weather classification, so new weather categories require only a new text prompt, with no labeled data and no retraining. Evaluated on 1327 DAWN images (YOLOv11m, IoU = 0.5, confidence = 0.25), CADENet achieves Recall = 0.0103 (micro), F1 = 0.0230 on snow, and F1 = 0.0038 on rain. We formalize the annotation completeness bias on DAWN-class data, so the reported F1 values are lower bounds on the true gain; recall is the annotation-gap-immune headline metric. Thread S sustains approximately 44 FPS regardless of enhancement load. No model retraining or additional sensor hardware is required.

AI.tldr: CADENet is a training-free, three-thread system for real-time object detection in adverse weather. It uses asynchronous enhancement (Thread Q) without blocking a fast detection pipeline (Thread S, ~44 FPS), plus CLIP-based zero-shot weather classification (Thread E). Results on DAWN dataset show low F1 (e.g., 0.023 on snow), which authors argue is a lower bound due to annotation completeness bias; they emphasize recall as a more reliable metric.
AI.motivation: Adverse weather degrades object detection in autonomous vehicles. Existing enhancement-then-detect approaches add latency and violate real-time requirements. Also, ground truth on degraded images is incomplete, causing an evaluation ceiling where useful enhancement shows little F1 gain.
AI.method: Three-thread asynchronous system: Thread S (YOLOv11n) runs detection at full frame rate; Thread Q applies condition-adaptive enhancement (CAPE) and fuses results via entropy-guided NMS (EG-NMS) without blocking Thread S; Thread E uses CLIP zero-shot weather classification, enabling new weather categories via text prompt without retraining.
AI.result: On 1327 DAWN images (YOLOv11m, IoU=0.5, conf=0.25): Recall=0.0103 (micro), F1=0.0230 on snow, F1=0.0038 on rain. Thread S sustains ~44 FPS regardless of enhancement load. No retraining or additional sensor hardware needed.
AI.conclusion: CADENet provides a real-time, training-free approach to adverse-weather detection. Reported F1 values are conservative lower bounds due to annotation bias; recall is the preferred metric. The system is extensible to new weather conditions via text prompts without retraining.

title: Robust Checkpoint Selection for Multimodal LLMs via Agentic Evaluation and Stability-Aware Ranking
summary: Checkpoint selection for multimodal large language models (MLLMs) presents significant challenges when performance differentials are marginal and evaluation signals are prone to noise. Existing methodologies rely heavily on static benchmarks or pointwise scoring, which frequently misalign with in-the-wild usage and lack robust uncertainty estimation, particularly in OCR-heavy scenarios. In this work, we formulate checkpoint selection as a robust decision problem under evaluation uncertainty. We propose a multi-stage framework that integrates curated real-world data, structured LLM-based judgment, and multi-stage ranking protocols. The evaluation system orchestrates progressive refinement via pointwise filtering, listwise ranking, and pairwise comparison. To enhance reliability, we introduce subsampling-based confidence estimation and a percentile-based scoring formulation that captures distributional characteristics while penalizing tail failures. Furthermore, we demonstrate that data quality, specifically OCR readability, is a critical determinant of evaluation validity.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a multi-stage framework for checkpoint selection in MLLMs that uses robust decision-making under evaluation uncertainty, integrating real-world data, LLM-based judgment, and multi-stage ranking (pointwise, listwise, pairwise) with subsampling confidence estimation and percentile-based scoring to handle marginal performance differences and noisy evaluation signals.
AI.motivation: Checkpoint selection for MLLMs is difficult when performance differences are small and evaluation signals are noisy. Existing methods using static benchmarks or pointwise scoring misalign with real-world usage and lack uncertainty estimation, especially in OCR-heavy scenarios.
AI.method: A multi-stage framework combining curated real-world data, structured LLM-based judgment, and multi-stage ranking protocols (pointwise filtering → listwise ranking → pairwise comparison). Uses subsampling-based confidence estimation and percentile-based scoring to capture distributional characteristics and penalize tail failures.
AI.result: Demonstrates that data quality, specifically OCR readability, is a critical determinant of evaluation validity. The framework provides robust uncertainty estimation and better alignment with in-the-wild usage compared to static benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-stage framework with confidence estimation and percentile-based scoring offers a more reliable approach to MLLM checkpoint selection under evaluation uncertainty, with OCR readability being a key factor for evaluation validity.

title: What Really Improves Mathematical Reasoning: Structured Reasoning Signals Beyond Pure Code
summary: Code has become a standard component of modern foundation language model (LM) training, yet its role beyond programming remains unclear. We revisit the claim that code improves reasoning through controlled pretraining experiments on a 10T-token corpus with fine-grained domain separation. Our findings are threefold. First, when code is restricted to standalone executable programs and Code-NL data are controlled for, code substantially improves programming ability but does not act as a general reasoning enhancer; instead, it competes with knowledge-intensive tasks, especially complex mathematical reasoning. Second, the reasoning gains often attributed to code are better explained by cross-domain structured reasoning traces, such as code-text and math-text mixtures, rather than by executable code alone. Third, increasing the density of structured math-domain samples within a fixed math budget yields substantial gains on difficult mathematical reasoning while largely preserving programming performance, suggesting that cognitive scaffolds offer a targeted way to mitigate cross-domain trade-offs. Finally, routing analyses show that data-composition effects are reflected in expert-activation patterns, providing mechanism-level evidence for competitive and synergistic interactions across domains. Our results clarify which data characteristics transfer across capability dimensions and point to more precise data-centric optimization strategies.

AI.tldr: Through controlled pretraining experiments on a 10T-token corpus, this paper finds that code improves programming but not general reasoning; reasoning gains come from structured reasoning traces (code-text, math-text mixtures), not executable code alone; increasing math-structured samples within fixed budget improves hard math reasoning while preserving programming performance.
AI.motivation: To clarify whether code in LM training truly enhances general reasoning abilities or if its benefits are domain-specific, given that code has become a standard component of modern foundation language model training.
AI.method: Controlled pretraining experiments on a 10T-token corpus with fine-grained domain separation, including routing analyses to examine expert-activation patterns.
AI.result: (1) Code improves programming but does not act as a general reasoning enhancer; it competes with knowledge-intensive tasks. (2) Reasoning gains attributed to code are better explained by cross-domain structured reasoning traces. (3) Increasing math-structured sample density within a fixed math budget improves hard math reasoning while preserving programming. (4) Data-composition effects are reflected in expert-activation patterns.
AI.conclusion: The results clarify which data characteristics transfer across capability dimensions and point to more precise data-centric optimization strategies for LM pretraining.

title: EventPrune: Cascaded Event-Assisted Token Pruning for Efficient First-Person Dynamic Spatial Reasoning
summary: First-person dynamic spatial reasoning requires models to track continuous motion and precise geometric structure, but the quadratic attention cost of Transformer-based Video-LLMs makes dense visual tokens computationally expensive. Existing token pruning paradigms predominantly rely on discrete static snapshots, failing to preserve the motion and geometric cues essential for reasoning. We propose Event Cascade Pruning (ECP), to our knowledge the first training-free framework that leverages the high-frequency motion cues from event cameras as a continuous event-guided motion prior to guide token selection. ECP combines three stages: Event-Triggered Causal Sampling to anchor motion-informative keyframes, Event-guided Motion Saliency Filtering to suppress event-inactive visual tokens, and Event-Attention Ranking Fusion to calibrate spatial attention with motion-salient dynamics. With 80% visual token reduction, ECP outperforms the full-token baseline (37.62% vs. 36.31%) while achieving 1.89x inference speedup and 52% GFLOPs reduction. We further introduce ESR-Real, the first real-world RGB-event benchmark for first-person spatial reasoning, where ECP improves accuracy by 2.68 percentage points over full-token baselines.

AI.tldr: ECP is a training-free token pruning framework using event camera motion priors to reduce 80% visual tokens while improving accuracy by 1.31% and achieving 1.89x speedup for first-person spatial reasoning.
AI.motivation: Quadratic attention cost of Transformer-based Video-LLMs makes dense visual tokens computationally expensive, and existing token pruning methods relying on discrete static snapshots fail to preserve motion and geometric cues essential for spatial reasoning.
AI.method: ECP combines three stages: Event-Triggered Causal Sampling (motion-informative keyframes), Event-guided Motion Saliency Filtering (suppress inactive tokens), and Event-Attention Ranking Fusion (calibrate spatial attention with motion-salient dynamics).
AI.result: With 80% token reduction, ECP outperforms full-token baseline (37.62% vs 36.31%) with 1.89x speedup and 52% GFLOPs reduction. On the new ESR-Real benchmark, ECP improves accuracy by 2.68 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: Event-guided motion priors effectively enable efficient token pruning while improving performance, demonstrating that motion-salient token selection is superior to conventional static approaches for dynamic spatial reasoning.

title: Robotics-Inspired Guardrails for Foundation Models in Socially Sensitive Domains
comment: Under review at Journal of Artificial Intelligence Research (JAIR)
summary: Foundation models are increasingly deployed in socially sensitive domains such as education, mental health, and caregiving, where failures are often cumulative and context-dependent. Existing guardrail approaches -- ranging from training-time alignment to prompting, decoding constraints, and post-hoc moderation -- primarily provide empirical risk reduction rather than enforceable behavioral guarantees, and largely treat safety as a property of individual outputs rather than interaction trajectories. We reframe guardrails as a problem of runtime behavioral control over interaction trajectories, drawing on robotics to introduce formal constructs for constraint enforcement in uncertain, closed-loop systems. We instantiate these ideas in the Grounded Observer framework and apply it across three real-world deployments: small talk, in-home autism therapy, and behavioral de-escalation in schools. Across settings, the framework enables runtime interventions that mitigate drift into undesirable interaction regimes while adapting to diverse social contexts. We discuss extensions to the framework and propose research directions toward stronger guarantees.

AI.tldr: This paper reframes AI safety guardrails from static output filtering to runtime behavioral control over entire interaction trajectories, drawing inspiration from robotics, and demonstrates a Grounded Observer framework that mitigates cumulative, context-dependent failures in sensitive domains like autism therapy and school de-escalation.
AI.motivation: Existing guardrail approaches treat safety as a property of individual outputs rather than interaction trajectories, but failures in socially sensitive domains (education, mental health, caregiving) are often cumulative and context-dependent, requiring enforceable behavioral guarantees over time.
AI.method: The authors reframe guardrails as runtime behavioral control over closed-loop interaction trajectories, borrowing formal constraint enforcement constructs from robotics. They instantiate this in the Grounded Observer framework and test it across three real-world deployments: small talk, in-home autism therapy, and behavioral de-escalation in schools.
AI.result: The Grounded Observer framework enables runtime interventions that mitigate drift into undesirable interaction regimes while adapting to diverse social contexts across all three deployment settings.
AI.conclusion: The paper proposes extending the framework and pursuing research directions toward stronger behavioral guarantees for AI systems in sensitive social domains.

title: INAR-VL: Input-Aware Routing for Edge-Cloud Vision-Language Inference
summary: Edge deployment of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) faces a tradeoff between latency and accuracy: cloud execution provides high-quality predictions but incurs communication delay and energy cost, while edge-only execution is faster but less accurate due to limited model capacity. This trade-off is further complicated by heterogeneity in image quality and reasoning complexity, making static placement suboptimal. We present INAR-VL, a lightweight edge-cloud routing system for multimodal inference in a two-tier deployment. INAR-VL maintains complementary VLMs across edge and cloud and uses lightweight image and text complexity signals to guide routing and model selection, executing simple queries locally while offloading complex ones when beneficial. Evaluation on visual question answering shows that INAR-VL executes 36% of requests on the edge, reduces latency by 24%, lowers energy by 26%, and preserves 97% of cloud-level accuracy.

AI.tldr: INAR-VL is a lightweight edge-cloud routing system for Vision-Language Models that uses image/text complexity signals to route simple queries to edge and complex ones to cloud, achieving 36% edge execution, 24% latency reduction, 26% energy savings while preserving 97% of cloud accuracy.
AI.motivation: Edge deployment of VLMs faces a tradeoff between latency/energy (edge) and accuracy (cloud), worsened by heterogeneity in image quality and reasoning complexity, making static placement suboptimal.
AI.method: INAR-VL uses lightweight image and text complexity signals to dynamically route simple queries to edge VLMs and complex queries to cloud VLMs, maintaining complementary models across both tiers.
AI.result: 36% of requests executed on edge, 24% latency reduction, 26% energy reduction, 97% of cloud-level accuracy preserved on visual question answering tasks.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic routing based on complexity signals effectively balances the accuracy-latency tradeoff for VLM edge deployment, enabling efficient two-tier inference.

title: GroupAffect-4: A Multimodal Dataset of Four-Person Collaborative Interaction
summary: Existing affective-computing, social-signal-processing, and meeting corpora capture important parts of human interaction, but they rarely support analysis of affect in co-located groups as a coupled individual, interpersonal, and group-level process. The required signals (per-participant physiology, eye movement, audio, self-report, task outcomes, and personality) are usually fragmented across separate dataset traditions. We introduce GroupAffect-4, a multimodal corpus of 40 participants in 10 four-person groups, each completing four ecologically varied collaborative tasks spanning information pooling, negotiation, idea generation, and a public-goods game. Each participant is instrumented with a wrist-worn physiology sensor, eye-tracking glasses, and a close-talk microphone; sessions include continuous affect self-reports, post-task questionnaires, task outcomes, and Big-Five personality scores, all time-aligned to a shared clock. The dataset covers over 91% of expected physiology windows and 98% of eye-tracking windows, with strong task validity confirmed by a clear affective manipulation check across the negotiation block. We define fifteen benchmarkable targets spanning three analysis levels -- within-person state, between-person traits, and group dynamics -- and report leave-one-group-out feasibility baselines establishing the dataset's evaluative scope. GroupAffect-4 is released with a BIDS-inspired structure, Croissant metadata, a datasheet, per-session quality reports, and open processing scripts. Code and processing scripts are available at https://github.com/meisamjam/GroupAffect-4; the dataset is publicly archived at https://zenodo.org/records/20037847.

AI.tldr: GroupAffect-4 is a multimodal corpus of 40 participants in 10 four-person groups performing four collaborative tasks, with synchronized physiology, eye-tracking, audio, self-report, and personality data, providing benchmarkable targets across individual, interpersonal, and group-level affect analysis.
AI.motivation: Existing corpora rarely support analysis of affect in co-located groups as a coupled individual, interpersonal, and group-level process, with required signals fragmented across separate dataset traditions.
AI.method: 40 participants in 10 four-person groups completed four collaborative tasks (information pooling, negotiation, idea generation, public-goods game). Each participant wore wrist physiology sensors, eye-tracking glasses, and close-talk microphones. Data includes continuous affect self-reports, post-task questionnaires, task outcomes, and Big-Five personality scores, all time-aligned.
AI.result: Dataset covers >91% physiology windows and >98% eye-tracking windows. Strong task validity confirmed via affective manipulation check. Fifteen benchmarkable targets defined across three levels (within-person, between-person, group dynamics) with leave-one-group-out feasibility baselines.
AI.conclusion: GroupAffect-4 provides a publicly available, well-structured multimodal dataset with quality reports and open processing scripts, enabling coupled analysis of affect across individual, interpersonal, and group levels.

title: Return of Frustratingly Easy Unsupervised Video Domain Adaptation
summary: Unsupervised video domain adaptation (UVDA) is a practical but under-explored problem. In this paper, we propose a frustratingly easy UVDA method, called MetaTrans. Specifically, MetaTrans adopts a concise learning objective that contains only two fundamental loss terms. Despite the simplicity of the learning objective, MetaTrans embodies an advanced UVDA idea, that is, handling the spatial and temporal divergence of cross-domain videos separately, through a subtle model architecture design. By implementing a temporal-static subtraction module, MetaTrans effectively removes spatial and temporal divergence. Extensive empirical evaluations, particularly on various cross-domain action recognition tasks, show substantial absolute adaptation performance enhancement and significantly superior relative performance gain compared with state-of-the-art UVDA baselines.

AI.tldr: MetaTrans is a simple yet effective unsupervised video domain adaptation method that achieves state-of-the-art results by using a temporal-static subtraction module to separately handle spatial and temporal divergence between domains.
AI.motivation: Unsupervised video domain adaptation (UVDA) is practical but under-explored, and existing methods are often complex.
AI.method: Uses a simple learning objective with only two loss terms, combined with a temporal-static subtraction module in the model architecture to separately handle spatial and temporal domain divergence.
AI.result: Substantial absolute adaptation performance enhancement and significantly superior relative performance gain compared to state-of-the-art UVDA baselines on cross-domain action recognition tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MetaTrans is an effective and simple UVDA method, demonstrating that handling spatial and temporal divergence separately through architectural design can yield strong results.

title: World-Ego Modeling for Long-Horizon Evolution in Hybrid Embodied Tasks
summary: World models are widely explored in embodied intelligence, yet they typically predict distinct evolutions of the world and the ego within a single stream, where the world captures persistent instruction-agnostic scene regularities and the ego captures robot-centric instruction-conditioned dynamics. This world-ego entanglement leads to a degradation in long-horizon embodied scenarios, particularly in hybrid tasks with interleaved navigation and manipulation behaviors. In this paper, we introduce \emph{World-Ego Modeling}, a new conceptual paradigm that decomposes future evolution into world and ego components. We define the world-ego boundary from three perspectives, i.e., motion-, semantic-, and intention-based views, and analyze three disentanglement strategies with post-, pre-, and full disentanglement. Further, we instantiate this paradigm as the World-Ego Model (WEM), a unified embodied world model that couples an implicit separate world-ego planner with a cascade-parallel mixture-of-experts (CP-MoE) diffusion generator. To enable rigorous evaluation, we further construct HTEWorld, the first benchmark for long-horizon world modeling with hybrid navigation-manipulation tasks, providing 125K video clips (over 4.5M frames) with fine-grained action annotations and 300 multi-turn evaluation trajectories (over 2K instructions). Extensive experiments show that WEM achieves state-of-the-art performance on HTEWorld while remaining competitive on existing manipulation-only benchmarks.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces World-Ego Modeling, a paradigm that separates world (instruction-agnostic scene regularities) from ego (instruction-conditioned dynamics) in embodied world models. The authors propose WEM, using a separate world-ego planner with CP-MoE diffusion, and release HTEWorld, a benchmark for hybrid navigation-manipulation tasks with 125K video clips and 300 evaluation trajectories.
AI.motivation: Existing world models entangle world (persistent scene regularities) and ego (robot-centric dynamics), causing degradation in long-horizon embodied scenarios, especially hybrid tasks that interleave navigation and manipulation.
AI.method: Proposes World-Ego Modeling paradigm with three disentanglement strategies (post-, pre-, full disentanglement) defined from motion, semantic, and intention perspectives. Instantiates as WEM: an implicit separate world-ego planner + cascade-parallel mixture-of-experts (CP-MoE) diffusion generator.
AI.result: WEM achieves state-of-the-art on HTEWorld benchmark and remains competitive on existing manipulation-only benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: World-ego disentanglement is crucial for long-horizon hybrid embodied tasks; the proposed paradigm and benchmark enable more effective world modeling for complex embodied AI.

title: Evaluating Memory Condensation Strategies for Coding Agents in Data-Driven Scientific Discovery
summary: Coding agents accumulate extensive context during long-running tasks, yet fixed context windows force practitioners to choose between truncation and task failure. While numerous memory condensation strategies have been proposed, from simple sliding windows to LLM-generated summaries, no systematic comparison exists to guide strategy selection, especially in scientific discovery tasks. We evaluate eight memory condensation strategies using GPT-4o on sixty DiscoveryBench tasks spanning six scientific domains (480 total evaluations). We find that no condenser significantly alters hypothesis quality, while LLM-based condensers increase token costs by 24-94 percent, and masking tool-call outputs achieves an 8.6 percent net savings. We also observe that the optimal condenser for data-driven scientific discovery varies by scientific domain and task length.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates eight memory condensation strategies for coding agents on 60 scientific discovery tasks across six domains (480 evaluations), finding that no condenser improves hypothesis quality, LLM-based methods increase token costs by 24-94%, and masking tool-call outputs saves 8.6% in tokens. Optimal strategies vary by domain and task length.
AI.motivation: Coding agents accumulate extensive context during long-running tasks, but fixed context windows force trade-offs between truncation and task failure. No systematic comparison of memory condensation strategies existed, especially for scientific discovery tasks.
AI.method: Evaluated eight memory condensation strategies using GPT-4o on sixty DiscoveryBench tasks across six scientific domains (480 total evaluations).
AI.result: No condenser significantly alters hypothesis quality. LLM-based condensers increase token costs by 24-94%. Masking tool-call outputs achieves 8.6% net token savings. Optimal condenser varies by scientific domain and task length.
AI.conclusion: Memory condensation strategies do not improve scientific hypothesis quality; LLM-based approaches are costly, while simple masking offers modest savings. Strategy selection should be domain- and task-specific.

title: Minimax Optimal Variance-Aware Regret Bounds for Multinomial Logistic MDPs
summary: We study reinforcement learning for episodic Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) whose transitions are modelled by a multinomial logistic (MNL) model. Existing algorithms for MNL mixture MDPs yield a regret of $\smash{\tilde{O}(dH^2\sqrt{T})}$ (Li et al., 2024), where $d$ is the feature dimension, $H$ the episode length, and $T$ the number of episodes. Inspired by the logistic bandit literature (Abeille et al., 2021; Faury et al., 2022; Boudart et al., 2026), we introduce a problem-dependent constant $\barσ\_T \leq 1/2$, measuring the normalised average variance of the optimal downstream value function along the learner's trajectory. We propose an algorithm achieving a regret of $\smash{\tilde{O}(dH^2\barσ\_T\sqrt{T})}$, which recovers the existing bound in the worst case and improves upon it for structured MDPs. For instance, for KL-constrained robust MDPs, $\barσ\_T = O(H^{-1})$, reducing the horizon dependence by a factor $H$. We further establish a matching $\smash{Ω(dH^2\barσ\_T\sqrt{T})}$ lower bound, proving minimax optimality (up to logarithmic factors) and fully characterising the regret complexity of MNL mixture MDPs for the first time.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a reinforcement learning algorithm for MNL mixture MDPs that achieves a regret bound of Õ(dH²σ̄_T√T), where σ̄_T captures problem-dependent variance. The bound improves upon prior work, especially for structured MDPs like robust MDPs, and is proven minimax optimal with a matching lower bound.
AI.motivation: Existing algorithms for MNL mixture MDPs yield worst-case regret bounds that ignore problem structure. The authors aim to derive a more refined, problem-dependent regret bound that can be significantly tighter for structured environments.
AI.method: The authors introduce a problem-dependent constant σ̄_T (normalised average variance of the optimal downstream value function along the learner's trajectory) and design an algorithm that achieves regret scaled by σ̄_T, leveraging insights from the logistic bandit literature.
AI.result: The algorithm achieves regret Õ(dH²σ̄_T√T), matching the existing worst-case bound and improving upon it for structured MDPs. Notably, for KL-constrained robust MDPs, σ̄_T = O(H⁻¹), reducing horizon dependence by a factor H. A matching Ω(dH²σ̄_T√T) lower bound is also established.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a full characterisation of the regret complexity of MNL mixture MDPs, proving minimax optimality up to logarithmic factors, and demonstrates the benefit of leveraging problem-dependent variance structures.

title: Are Watermarked Images Editable? SafeMark for Watermark-Preserving Text-Guided Image Editing
summary: This paper investigates a fundamental yet underexplored question: can watermarked images remain editable without compromising watermark integrity? We propose SafeMark, a framework for watermark-preserving text-guided image manipulation that explicitly integrates watermark integrity into the editing process. Specifically, SafeMark adds a thresholded watermark-decoding loss directly to the diffusion editor's training objective, fine-tuning the editor so that semantically valid edits also preserve the embedded watermark at the final output. This design admits a clean information-theoretic justification: maintaining high bit-accuracy on the edited image lower-bounds the mutual information that the editor channel preserves between watermark and edited output, the quantity that fundamentally controls watermark recoverability. SafeMark is compatible with differentiable diffusion-based editors, and requires no architectural modification. Extensive evaluations across multiple datasets, text-guided editing methods, and post-edit distortion settings demonstrate that SafeMark achieves high watermark bit accuracy across diverse editing settings while maintaining high-quality semantic edits, without sacrificing robustness to common post-edit distortions. These results demonstrate that semantic editability and watermark integrity are fundamentally compatible, enabling trustworthy image provenance in generative editing pipelines.

AI.tldr: SafeMark is a framework that enables watermarked images to remain editable (via text-guided diffusion-based editing) without compromising the embedded watermark, by adding a watermark-decoding loss to the editor's training objective.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether watermarked images can be semantically edited while preserving watermark integrity—a fundamental but underexplored question for trustworthy image provenance.
AI.method: SafeMark integrates a thresholded watermark-decoding loss into the diffusion editor's training objective, fine-tuning the editor so semantically valid edits also preserve the embedded watermark. The approach has an information-theoretic justification and is compatible with differentiable diffusion-based editors without architectural changes.
AI.result: Across multiple datasets, editing methods, and post-edit distortions, SafeMark achieves high watermark bit accuracy and high-quality semantic edits, while maintaining robustness to common distortions.
AI.conclusion: Semantic editability and watermark integrity are fundamentally compatible, enabling trustworthy image provenance in generative editing pipelines.

title: Probing Embodied LLMs: When Higher Observation Fidelity Hurts Problem Solving
comment: Submitted to From Animals to Animats: The 18th International Conference on the Simulation of Adaptive Behavior (SAB)
summary: Large Language Models are increasingly proposed as cognitive components for robotic systems, yet their opaque decision processes make it difficult to explain success or failure in closed-loop embodied tasks. Following an empirical AI methodology, we study embodied LLM agents behaviorally by varying the information available to the agent and measuring the resulting changes in behavior. Using the Lockbox, a sequential mechanical puzzle with hidden interdependencies, we evaluate LLMs across RGB, RGB-D, and ground-truth symbolic observations in a physical robotic setup and use controlled simulation to probe the resulting behavior. Counterintuitively, agents perform best under raw RGB input and worst under perfect ground-truth observations. In simulation, we probe this effect by randomly flipping perceived action outcomes and find that moderate noise improves performance, peaking at a 40% flip probability with a 2.85-fold success rate increase over the noise-free baseline. Further analysis links this gain to a reduction in repetitive action loops. These findings suggest that success rates alone are insufficient for evaluating LLMs, as measured performance may reflect the interaction between perceptual errors and reasoning failures rather than robust problem solving.

AI.tldr: LLM-based robotic agents perform counterintuitively better under noisy raw RGB input than under perfect ground-truth observations, with moderate perceptual noise improving success rates by up to 2.85x by reducing repetitive action loops.
AI.motivation: LLMs are being used as cognitive components in robotics, but their opaque decision processes make it hard to explain success/failure in embodied tasks. The authors aim to study LLM behavior empirically by manipulating information available to the agent.
AI.method: Using a Lockbox puzzle, they evaluate LLMs under RGB, RGB-D, and ground-truth symbolic observations in both physical robotic and simulated setups. They probe the noise effect by randomly flipping perceived action outcomes in simulation.
AI.result: Raw RGB input yields best performance while perfect ground-truth yields worst. Moderate noise (40% flip probability) increases success rate 2.85-fold over noise-free baseline. This gain is linked to reduced repetitive action loops.
AI.conclusion: Success rates alone are insufficient for evaluating LLMs; measured performance may reflect interaction between perceptual errors and reasoning failures rather than robust problem solving.

title: Delta Attention Residuals
summary: Attention Residuals replace standard additive residual connections with learned softmax attention over previous layer outputs, enabling selective cross-layer routing. However, standard Attention Residuals still attend over cumulative hidden states in previous layers, which are highly redundant. We show that this redundancy leads to routing collapse in deeper layers: attention weights become low-contrast and closer to uniform (max weight ${\approx}$0.2), limiting the model's ability to select informative states in previous layers. This raises a key but underexplored design question: what layer-wise representations should be routed in Attention Residuals? To answer this question, we propose Delta Attention Residuals, which attend over deltas -- the change introduced by each sublayer ($\mathbf{v}_i = \mathbf{h}_{i+1} - \mathbf{h}_i$) -- instead of cumulative states. Delta representations are structurally diverse and yield higher-contrast attention distributions (max weight ${\approx}$0.6), enabling more selective and effective routing across layers. This principle applies at both per-sublayer and block granularity. Across all tested scales (220M--7.6B), Delta Attention Residuals consistently outperform both standard residuals and Attention Residuals, with 1.7--8.2\% validation perplexity gains. Delta Attention Residuals also enables converting pretrained checkpoints into Delta Attention Residuals via standard fine-tuning. Code is available at https://github.com/wdlctc/delta-attention-residuals-code.

AI.tldr: Delta Attention Residuals improve upon standard Attention Residuals by routing over layer-wise deltas (differences between consecutive hidden states) instead of cumulative states, achieving higher-contrast attention weights and 1.7-8.2% perplexity gains across 220M-7.6B scale models.
AI.motivation: Standard Attention Residuals attend over cumulative hidden states across layers, which are highly redundant. This causes routing collapse in deeper layers (attention weights become near-uniform with max weight ~0.2), limiting the model's ability to selectively route informative states.
AI.method: Delta Attention Residuals attend over deltas (v_i = h_{i+1} - h_i) instead of cumulative hidden states, where delta represents the change introduced by each sublayer. This applies at both per-sublayer and block granularity, and can be adapted from pretrained checkpoints via fine-tuning.
AI.result: Across 220M-7.6B parameter scales, Delta Attention Residuals consistently outperform both standard residuals and Attention Residuals, achieving 1.7-8.2% validation perplexity gains. The delta representations yield higher-contrast attention distributions (max weight ~0.6 vs ~0.2 for standard Attention Residuals).
AI.conclusion: Delta representations are structurally diverse and enable more selective and effective routing across layers. The method also supports converting pretrained checkpoints via fine-tuning, making it practical for existing models.

title: OpenComputer: Verifiable Software Worlds for Computer-Use Agents
summary: We present OpenComputer, a verifier-grounded framework for constructing verifiable software worlds for computer-use agents. OpenComputer integrates four components: (1) app-specific state verifiers that expose structured inspection endpoints over real applications, (2) a self-evolving verification layer that improves verifier reliability using execution-grounded feedback, (3) a task-generation pipeline that synthesizes realistic and machine-checkable desktop tasks, and (4) an evaluation harness that records full trajectories and computes auditable partial-credit rewards. In its current form, OpenComputer covers 33 desktop applications and 1,000 finalized tasks spanning browsers, office tools, creative software, development environments, file managers, and communication applications. Experiments show that OpenComputer's hard-coded verifiers align more closely with human adjudication than LLM-as-judge evaluation, especially when success depends on fine-grained application state. Frontier agents struggle with end-to-end completion despite partial progress, and open-source models exhibit sharp drops from their OSWorld-Verified scores, exposing a persistent gap in robust computer automation.

AI.tldr: OpenComputer is a framework that creates verifiable software environments for computer-use agents, using app-specific verifiers, self-evolving verification, task generation, and evaluation harness. It covers 33 apps and 1,000 tasks, showing that hard-coded verifiers outperform LLM-as-judge, and that even frontier agents struggle with end-to-end tasks.
AI.motivation: To create a reliable, verifiable framework for evaluating computer-use agents, addressing the lack of structured, machine-checkable evaluation in real desktop environments.
AI.method: Four-component framework: (1) app-specific state verifiers with structured inspection endpoints over real apps, (2) self-evolving verification layer using execution-grounded feedback, (3) task-generation pipeline for realistic machine-checkable desktop tasks, (4) evaluation harness recording trajectories with partial-credit rewards. Covers 33 desktop applications and 1,000 tasks.
AI.result: Hard-coded verifiers align better with human judgment than LLM-as-judge, especially for fine-grained state. Frontier agents show partial progress but struggle with end-to-end completion. Open-source models drop sharply from OSWorld-Verified scores.
AI.conclusion: OpenComputer enables more reliable agent evaluation in real desktop environments, revealing a persistent gap in robust computer automation that current models cannot close.

title: iDiff: Interpretable Difference-aware Framework for Pairwise Image Quality Assessment
summary: Pairwise image quality assessment (IQA) in professional photography requires a model not only to identify the preferred image between two candidates, but also to provide convincing and image-grounded reasoning. In the NTIRE 2026 RAIM challenge, this requirement is further emphasized by jointly evaluating preference prediction and rationale generation. To address this task, we propose iDiff, an Interpretable Difference-aware framework for pairwise image quality assessment. Our method adopts a dual-branch design consisting of an Answer Model and a Thinking Model. The Answer Model performs robust preference prediction by explicitly decomposing each sample into left/right global and local views, followed by content-aware specialization for person and scene images and ensemble-based aggregation across backbones. The Thinking Model focuses on rationale generation and is progressively enhanced with expert-style templates, multi-source quality features, and answer-aware supervision conditioned on the Answer Model prediction. In this way, iDiff jointly models discriminative decision making and structured explanation, improving both robustness and interpretability. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework on both accuracy and reasoning-quality metrics. Our method achieved first place in the NTIRE 2026 RAIM challenge, showing the effectiveness of integrating explicit difference modeling with structured multimodal reasoning for pairwise IQA.

AI.tldr: iDiff is a dual-branch framework (Answer Model + Thinking Model) for pairwise image quality assessment that jointly predicts image preference and generates interpretable rationales, achieving first place in the NTIRE 2026 RAIM challenge.
AI.motivation: Professional pairwise IQA requires not only identifying the preferred image but also providing convincing, image-grounded reasoning. Existing methods often lack interpretability and fail to jointly model discriminative decisions with structured explanations.
AI.method: iDiff adopts a dual-branch design: (1) An Answer Model that decomposes samples into left/right global and local views, uses content-aware specialization for person/scene images, and ensemble aggregation; (2) A Thinking Model enhanced with expert-style templates, multi-source quality features, and answer-aware supervision from the Answer Model for rationale generation.
AI.result: Achieved first place in the NTIRE 2026 RAIM challenge, demonstrating effectiveness on both accuracy and reasoning-quality metrics.
AI.conclusion: Integrating explicit difference modeling with structured multimodal reasoning significantly improves both robustness and interpretability for pairwise IQA.

title: SPHERICAL KV: Angle-Domain Attention and Rate-Distortion Retention for Efficient Long-Context Inference
summary:
Long-context inference is increasingly constrained by the KV cache: resident memory grows with context length, and decoding becomes limited by repeated High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) streaming rather than arithmetic. Existing methods such as eviction, windowing, quantization, and offloading reduce footprint, but often leave the critical-path bottleneck only partially addressed, especially when compressed states must still be reconstructed into dense vectors during decoding.
  We present Spherical KV, a long-context inference method that treats KV allocation as a rate-distortion problem grounded in attention geometry for efficient decoding. The method is built on two ideas: (i) represent directional information cheaply in the decode hot loop, and (ii) allocate retention and precision according to estimated future utility. Its first component, Angle-Domain Attention (ADA), stores keys in a spherical parameterization consisting of a scalar radius and compact angle codes, and computes attention logits directly from these codes without reconstructing dense keys. This preserves a paged, block-local, fusion-friendly decode path and directly targets HBM traffic in realistic serving settings. Its second component, Rate-Distortion Retention (RDR), jointly chooses keep/drop decisions and precision tiers per token and head under a fixed budget, producing tier-homogeneous pages with lightweight metadata and coalesced reads. Together, ADA and RDR provide a deployment-oriented mechanism for reducing KV residency while preserving decode efficiency.

AI.tldr: Spherical KV reduces KV cache memory and HBM traffic for long-context LLM inference by storing keys in spherical coordinates (ADA) and jointly optimizing retention/precision under a fixed budget (RDR), enabling efficient decoding without reconstructing dense vectors.
AI.motivation: Long-context inference is bottlenecked by KV cache growth — memory scales with context length, and decoding is limited by repeated HBM streaming rather than compute. Existing methods (eviction, windowing, quantization, offloading) reduce footprint but often leave the critical decode path bottleneck partially unaddressed, especially when compressed states must be reconstructed into dense vectors during decoding.
AI.method: Two components: (i) Angle-Domain Attention (ADA) — stores keys in spherical parameterization (scalar radius + compact angle codes), computes attention logits directly from codes without reconstructing dense keys, preserving paged block-local fusion-friendly decode. (ii) Rate-Distortion Retention (RDR) — jointly selects keep/drop decisions and precision tiers per token and head under a fixed budget, producing tier-homogeneous pages with lightweight metadata and coalesced reads.
AI.result: The method reduces KV residency while preserving decode efficiency. ADA directly targets HBM traffic in realistic serving settings by avoiding dense key reconstruction. RDR enables tier-homogeneous pages with coalesced reads under budget constraints.
AI.conclusion: Spherical KV provides a deployment-oriented mechanism for reducing KV cache footprint while maintaining decode performance, formulated as a rate-distortion problem grounded in attention geometry.

title: Distribution-Free Uncertainty Quantification for Continuous AI Agent Evaluation
comment: 6 pages, 7 figures, 2 tables. Accepted at the ICML 2026 Workshop on Agentic Uncertainty Quantification (AgenticUQ) - Poster
summary: We adapt split conformal prediction and adaptive conformal inference (ACI) to continuous AI agent evaluation, providing distribution-free coverage guarantees for forecasted quality scores. Conformal intervals achieve calibration error below 0.02 across all nominal levels at the 24h horizon, while ACI correctly widens intervals by 35% following agent releases then reconverges. We further develop compositional uncertainty bounds for multi-agent pipelines (validated via simulation across inter-stage correlations rho in [-0.5, 0.9]), a conformal abstention rule for pairwise rankings with controlled false-ranking rate, and FDR-corrected abstention for leaderboard-scale multiple testing. Evaluating 50 agents via 18 real-time signals collected hourly, we show that per-agent conditional coverage is well-concentrated around the nominal level (mean 80.4%, 90% of agents within [72%, 90%]), and that cross-source sentiment divergence predicts ranking instability (r=0.64, p<0.01). A circularity-controlled validation confirms the framework captures signal beyond benchmarks (rho_s=0.52, p<0.01, n=35). Code and data are released under CC BY 4.0.

AI.tldr: This paper adapts conformal prediction and ACI to provide distribution-free uncertainty quantification for continuous AI agent evaluation, achieving well-calibrated prediction intervals, compositional bounds for multi-agent pipelines, and FDR-controlled abstention rules for rankings.
AI.motivation: To provide rigorous, distribution-free uncertainty guarantees for evaluating AI agents' quality scores over time, addressing challenges like model updates, multi-agent pipelines, and large-scale leaderboard comparisons.
AI.method: Adapts split conformal prediction and adaptive conformal inference (ACI) for time-series agent evaluation; develops compositional uncertainty bounds for multi-agent pipelines via simulation; proposes conformal abstention rule for pairwise rankings and FDR-corrected abstention for leaderboard-scale testing.
AI.result: Calibration error below 0.02 across nominal levels at 24h horizon; ACI intervals widen by 35% post-release then reconverge; per-agent coverage concentrated around 80.4% (90% of agents within [72%,90%]); cross-source sentiment divergence predicts ranking instability (r=0.64, p<0.01); circularity-controlled validation shows signal beyond benchmarks (rho_s=0.52, p<0.01).
AI.conclusion: The proposed conformal framework provides reliable, coverage-guaranteed uncertainty quantification for continuous AI agent evaluation, validated across extensive real-time data and simulations.

title: Dual-Prompt CLIP with Hybrid Visual Encoders for Occluded Person Re-Identification
summary: Occluded person re-identification focuses on matching partially visible pedestrians across multiple camera views. However, occlusions disrupt body-region cues, thereby complicating cross-view matching. Most person ReID methods built on pretrained vision-language models only focus on enhancing prompt-based feature learning while ignoring the semantic information of occluders. Based on the success of CLIP-ReID, we propose a novel Dual Prompt Learning ReID (DPL-ReID) model for occluded person ReID. It incorporates a Dual Prompt Learning (Dual-PL) strategy, which can utilize textual cues to capture complete pedestrian semantics and keep robustness against occlusion, and a Real-World Occlusion Augmentation (RWOA) method that realistically simulates occlusion scenarios encountered in real word to enrich occluded samples. In addition, we also design a Weighted Gated Feature Fusion (WGFF) method, which in corporates LSNet to capture global information and act as a feature-gating mechanism. This mechanism can effectively guide the CLIP visual encoder toward generating more comprehensive feature representations. Extensive experiments on several benchmark occluded ReID datasets show that our proposed DPL-ReID achieves the state-of-the art performance. The occlusion instance library are available at https://github.com/stone-qiao/DPL-ReID.

AI.tldr: DPL-ReID is a new method for occluded person re-identification that uses dual prompt learning, real-world occlusion augmentation, and weighted gated feature fusion to achieve state-of-the-art performance on benchmark datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing person ReID methods built on vision-language models (like CLIP-ReID) focus only on prompt-based feature learning but ignore semantic information of occluders, making them vulnerable to occlusion disruptions in cross-view matching.
AI.method: Three key components: (1) Dual Prompt Learning (Dual-PL) strategy using textual cues for complete pedestrian semantics; (2) Real-World Occlusion Augmentation (RWOA) for realistic occlusion simulation; (3) Weighted Gated Feature Fusion (WGFF) with LSNet for feature gating to guide CLIP visual encoder.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on several benchmark occluded ReID datasets show state-of-the-art performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DPL-ReID effectively handles occluded person re-identification by leveraging dual prompt learning, realistic occlusion augmentation, and gated feature fusion, outperforming prior methods.

title: When Individually Calibrated Models Become Collectively Miscalibrated
comment: 42 pages, 1 main figure, multiple tables. Accepted at ProbML 2026
summary:
Probabilistic prediction systems often aggregate probability estimates from multiple models into a single decision. A common assumption is that if each model is individually calibrated, the aggregate prediction will also be well calibrated. We show that this assumption fails in multi-agent settings: individually calibrated predictors can become collectively miscalibrated when their predictions interact strategically, in the game-theoretic sense of Brier-optimal local response, even without deliberate coordination.
  This phenomenon arises naturally when agents are independently trained on overlapping data. We prove that under Brier-score-based aggregation with positively correlated beliefs, each agent's individually optimal report systematically underestimates the positive-class probability, yielding a Price of Anarchy greater than one whenever Cov(b_i, b_j) > 0.
  In a canonical setting (n = 5 agents, pairwise correlation = 0.5, base rate = 0.3), the empirically measured PoA in false-negative rate reaches 7.25x. In contrast, VCG-based aggregation aligns incentives by rewarding marginal contribution, achieving dominant-strategy incentive compatibility and near-optimal performance.
  Experiments on three real-world datasets (NSL-KDD, UNSW-NB15, Credit Card Fraud) show that VCG provides strong robustness while maintaining comparable accuracy. It performs particularly well in data-sparse and adversarial settings, and adaptive weighting further improves performance under distribution shift.

AI.tldr: Individually calibrated probabilistic predictors become collectively miscalibrated under strategic interaction (Brier-optimal local responses), leading to systematic underestimation of positive-class probability and a Price of Anarchy >1. VCG-based aggregation solves this by aligning incentives, achieving dominant-strategy incentive compatibility and near-optimal performance.
AI.motivation: To challenge the common assumption that aggregating individually calibrated models preserves calibration, showing that strategic (game-theoretic) interactions among predictors cause collective miscalibration even without coordination.
AI.method: The authors prove theoretically that under Brier-score aggregation with positively correlated beliefs, individually optimal reports systematically underestimate positive-class probability. They compare Brier aggregation with VCG-based aggregation, and validate empirically on three datasets (NSL-KDD, UNSW-NB15, Credit Card Fraud).
AI.result: Price of Anarchy in false-negative rate reaches 7.25x (n=5, correlation=0.5, base rate=0.3). VCG aggregation achieves dominant-strategy incentive compatibility, strong robustness, comparable accuracy, and superior performance in data-sparse and adversarial settings.
AI.conclusion: VCG-based aggregation effectively resolves collective miscalibration from strategic behavior, and adaptive weighting further improves performance under distribution shift.

title: From SGD to Muon: Adaptive Optimization via Schatten-p Norms
summary: Modern optimizers, like Muon, impose matrix-wise geometry constraints on their updates. These matrix-wise constraints can be unified under Linear Minimization Oracle (LMO) theory. However, all current methods impose fixed LMO geometries for the update rules, chosen by-design or empirically, which are not necessarily optimal according to the problem's geometry. We introduce a novel efficient datadriven criterion for dynamically choosing proxy-optimal update LMO geometries on individual Deep Neural Network layers. Derived in closed form from gradient and activation statistics using a single-step random feature regression surrogate model, our criterion navigates a design space interpolating from SGD to Muon updates. Moreover, integrating parameter-wise preconditioning allows our framework to recover SGD, Muon, Adam, and MuAdam as specific extrema. To make this adaptive approach scalable, we pair it with efficient computational strategies, achieving only a $\sim$ 3% runtime overhead on highly optimized baselines. As a proof of concept, we show that this data-driven optimizer beats or remains competitive with the performance of the best performing optimizer between Muon and AdamW across three different training scenarios. Ultimately, this work provides evidence that LMO geometry can be successfully and efficiently adapted from runtime data, opening a new pathway for optimizer design beyond static geometries.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a data-driven method to dynamically choose optimal update geometries for optimizers like Muon, using a closed-form criterion from gradient/activation statistics, achieving adaptive LMO selection with only ~3% runtime overhead and competitive performance against Muon and AdamW.
AI.motivation: Current optimizers use fixed, hand-chosen matrix-wise geometries (LMO) for updates, which may not be optimal for the specific problem geometry encountered during training.
AI.method: A data-driven criterion, derived in closed-form via a single-step random feature regression surrogate model from gradient and activation statistics, dynamically selects proxy-optimal LMO geometries per layer, interpolating from SGD to Muon-like updates, with efficient computational strategies.
AI.result: The adaptive optimizer achieves only ~3% runtime overhead over highly optimized baselines and beats or remains competitive with the best static optimizer (Muon or AdamW) across three training scenarios.
AI.conclusion: LMO geometry can be successfully and efficiently adapted from runtime data, opening a new pathway for optimizer design beyond static geometries.

title: Towards Camera-Robust 3D Localization: Equation-Anchored Tool-Use for MLLMs
summary: 3D localization in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), including 3D object detection and 3D visual grounding, is fundamentally limited by camera intrinsic ambiguity: the same image admits different 3D scenes under different cameras. Existing MLLMs either ignore camera parameters and overfit to a canonical training intrinsic, or retrieve depth and 3D cues from external tools but treat the returned values as reference cues (numerical hints that the model is free to interpret implicitly), both preventing camera information from being deterministically propagated into the prediction. We propose an equation-anchored tool-use framework that re-purposes spatial tools as formula variables. The proposed framework proactively retrieves camera intrinsics and samples multi-point metric depths, writes the pinhole back-projection equation $\hat{X} = (u_c - c_x)\bar{Z}/f_x$ explicitly in Chain-of-Thought (CoT), and substitutes tool outputs into the formula before regressing the final 9-DoF bounding box. On both 3D object detection and 3D visual grounding tasks under rescaled camera intrinsics from $0.5\times$ to $1.5\times$, our method outperforms RGB-only and tool-augmented baselines, with significant gains where the camera deviates most from the training scale. Code and data will be released.

AI.tldr: Proposes an equation-anchored tool-use framework for 3D localization in MLLMs that writes explicit pinhole back-projection equations in CoT, using camera intrinsics and multi-point metric depths as formula variables to resolve camera intrinsic ambiguity.
AI.motivation: 3D localization in MLLMs suffers from camera intrinsic ambiguity—same image can correspond to different 3D scenes under different cameras. Existing methods either ignore camera parameters (overfitting to canonical intrinsics) or treat retrieved depth as implicit reference cues, failing to propagate camera information deterministically.
AI.method: Proposes an equation-anchored tool-use framework that proactively retrieves camera intrinsics and samples multi-point metric depths, writes explicit pinhole back-projection equation (X = (u_c - c_x)Z/f_x) in Chain-of-Thought, and substitutes tool outputs into the formula before regressing the final 9-DoF bounding box.
AI.result: Outperforms RGB-only and tool-augmented baselines on both 3D object detection and 3D visual grounding under rescaled camera intrinsics (0.5x to 1.5x), with largest gains when camera deviates most from training scale.
AI.conclusion: Explicit, deterministic integration of camera geometry via symbolic equations in CoT significantly improves 3D localization robustness to camera intrinsic variations, and the framework generalizes across both detection and grounding tasks.

title: TwinRouterBench: Fast Static and Live Dynamic Evaluation for Realistic Agentic LLM Routing
summary: LLM routing matters most in long-horizon applications such as coding agents, deep research systems, and computer-use agents, where a single user request triggers many model calls. Routing each call to the cheapest sufficient model can cut costs without sacrificing quality, yet existing router benchmarks evaluate routers only on one-shot prompts. They never expose the router-visible prefix at an intermediate agent step, never test whether a cheaper replacement preserves downstream task success, and often rely on online LLM judges at evaluation time. We introduce TwinRouterBench, a step-level routing benchmark with two tracks. The static track provides 970 router-visible prefixes from 520 instances across SWE-bench, BFCL, mtRAG, QMSum, and PinchBench, each paired with an execution-verified target tier estimated under a released downgrade-and-cascade protocol; scoring is deterministic arithmetic over tier labels, trajectory membership, and token costs, with no online evaluator-side LLM judge. The dynamic track supplies a harness that runs routers on the full 500-case SWE-bench Verified suite; in this paper we report a 100-case held-out evaluation disjoint from the static SWE supervision split. At each LLM call the router selects a concrete model from a locked pool, and success is measured by official task resolution and realized API spend. The two tracks support fast offline iteration followed by end-to-end validation under live agent execution. Code and data are available at https://github.com/CommonstackAI/TwinRouterBench.

AI.tldr: TwinRouterBench is a step-level LLM routing benchmark with static (offline, deterministic scoring) and dynamic (live agent execution) tracks, designed to evaluate routers on long-horizon agent tasks rather than one-shot prompts.
AI.motivation: Existing router benchmarks only evaluate on one-shot prompts, ignoring the multi-step nature of coding agents, deep research, and computer-use agents where routing decisions at intermediate steps matter for cost-quality trade-offs.
AI.method: Two-track benchmark: (1) Static track with 970 router-visible prefixes from 520 instances across 5 benchmarks, using a downgrade-and-cascade protocol with deterministic arithmetic scoring. (2) Dynamic track with a harness running routers on SWE-bench Verified (100-case held-out) with a locked model pool, measuring task resolution and API cost.
AI.result: Static track provides 970 prefixes paired with execution-verified target tiers. Dynamic track reports results on a 100-case held-out SWE-bench Verified subset, using official task resolution and realized API spend as metrics.
AI.conclusion: TwinRouterBench enables fast offline iteration (static track) followed by end-to-end validation under live execution (dynamic track), addressing the gap in step-level routing evaluation for long-horizon agent tasks.

title: Prior Knowledge or Search? A Study of LLM Agents in Hardware-Aware Code Optimization
summary: LLM discovery and optimization systems are increasingly applied across domains, implementing a common propose-evaluate-revise loop. Such optimization or discovery progresses via context conditioning on received feedback from an environment. However, as modern LLM agents are increasingly complex in their structure, it is difficult to evaluate which components contribute the most, and when and how this exploration may fail. We answer these questions through three controlled experiments. Our findings: (1) In pure black-box optimization, LLMs act as greedy optimizers. (2) In zero-shot kernel generation, providing explicit input-size information has no measurable effect, models converge to the same kernel parameters regardless of size or temperature, as though the size instruction were invisible. Moreover, when tasked to perform kernel optimization for uncommon kernel sizes, performance sharply degrades regardless of the language used. (3) In feedback-loop kernel optimization, CUDA improves monotonically under iterative feedback, while TVM IR actively degrades, which demonstrates that kernel optimization degrades when models operate with low-density language. Our results conclude that LLMs in code optimization tasks highly depend on pretrained priors rather than provided feedback or agentic structure.

AI.tldr: LLMs in code optimization act as greedy optimizers heavily reliant on pretrained priors, with performance degrading when operating in low-density languages or uncommon configurations, while explicit instructions like input-size have no measurable effect.
AI.motivation: To evaluate which components contribute most in LLM-based optimization systems and understand when and how exploration may fail, given the increasing complexity of LLM agent structures.
AI.method: Three controlled experiments: (1) pure black-box optimization, (2) zero-shot kernel generation with varied input-size information, (3) feedback-loop kernel optimization comparing CUDA vs TVM IR.
AI.result: (1) LLMs behave as greedy optimizers in black-box tasks. (2) Input-size info is invisible; performance sharply degrades for uncommon kernel sizes regardless of language. (3) CUDA improves under iterative feedback while TVM IR degrades, showing low-density language causes degradation.
AI.conclusion: LLMs in code optimization tasks highly depend on pretrained priors rather than provided feedback or agentic structure.

title: Boosting Text-to-Image Diffusion Models via Core Token Attention-Based Seed Selection
summary: Text-to-image diffusion models can synthesize high-quality images, yet the outcome is notoriously sensitive to the random seed: different initial seeds often yield large variations in image quality and prompt-image alignment. We revisit this "seed effect" and show that attention dynamics over prompt core tokens, the content-bearing words, measured during the first few denoising steps, strongly predict final generation quality. Building on this observation, we introduce Attention-Based Seed Selection (ABSS), a training-free, plug-and-play method that ranks seeds for a given prompt by leveraging cross-attention to core tokens during the denoising process. ABSS requires no finetuning and does not alter the initial noise; it scores and ranks all candidate seeds, keeps only the top-k for full generation, and discards the rest, without relying on a fixed accept/reject threshold. Operating purely at inference time, ABSS can serve as a lightweight pre-selection add-on for existing seed-optimization pipelines, enabling additional gains. Across three benchmarks, extensive experiments show that ABSS enables consistent improvements in text-image alignment and visual quality for Stable Diffusion variants, as corroborated by human preference and alignment metrics.

AI.tldr: ABSS is a training-free method that selects better random seeds for text-to-image diffusion models by analyzing cross-attention to core tokens during early denoising steps, improving generation quality without modifying the model or noise.
AI.motivation: To address the sensitivity of text-to-image diffusion models to random seeds, where different seeds cause large variations in image quality and prompt alignment, by understanding and leveraging attention dynamics as a predictive signal.
AI.method: ABSS (Attention-Based Seed Selection) scores and ranks candidate seeds by measuring cross-attention to core (content-bearing) tokens during early denoising steps, keeps top-k seeds for full generation, and discards the rest—requiring no training or fixed thresholds.
AI.result: ABSS consistently improves text-image alignment and visual quality across three benchmarks for Stable Diffusion variants, as validated by human preference and alignment metrics.
AI.conclusion: Attention dynamics over prompt core tokens during early denoising steps strongly predict final generation quality, and ABSS serves as an effective, lightweight pre-selection add-on for existing seed-optimization pipelines.

title: From Llama to Cria: Scaling Down Neural Networks via Neuron-Level Spectral Structural Importance Evaluation
summary: This paper proposes a neuron pruning framework based on neuron-level spectral structural importance evaluation. Given a trained neural network, we record the hidden states of each hidden layer during inference and model neurons as graph nodes, with hidden states treated as graph signals. Using ideas from graph signal processing, we infer layer-wise input and output graphs that characterize the structural relationships among neurons before and after each layer transformation. We then evaluate the spectral structural importance of neurons by analyzing the transformation between these graphs based on spectral graph theory. Neurons with high spectral structural importance are regarded as strongly involved in the internal representation transformation and are therefore preserved, while neurons with low importance scores are selected as pruning candidates. The pruning process is conducted iteratively until a predefined effective parameter reduction target is reached. Instead of fine-tuning after every pruning step, the proposed strategy first removes low-importance neurons to obtain a compact architecture and then applies a final recovery fine-tuning stage to restore task performance. By connecting neuron pruning with graph signal processing and spectral structural analysis, the proposed framework offers a principled way to reduce neural network size while maintaining solution quality. Experimental results on CIFAR-10 image classification and SST-2 sentiment classification show that our method can effectively remove low-importance neurons and achieve compact networks with competitive performance after recovery fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: A neuron pruning framework using spectral structural importance evaluation based on graph signal processing, which removes low-importance neurons iteratively before a final fine-tuning stage.
AI.motivation: To reduce neural network size by identifying and removing structurally unimportant neurons, leveraging spectral graph theory to evaluate neuron importance based on their role in internal representation transformation.
AI.method: Records hidden states of each layer during inference, models neurons as graph nodes with hidden states as graph signals, infers layer-wise input/output graphs using graph signal processing, evaluates spectral structural importance via spectral graph theory, iteratively prunes low-importance neurons, then applies a final recovery fine-tuning stage.
AI.result: Experiments on CIFAR-10 (image classification) and SST-2 (sentiment classification) show effective removal of low-importance neurons, achieving compact networks with competitive performance after recovery fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework offers a principled, graph-theoretic approach to neural network pruning that effectively reduces model size while maintaining performance, without requiring fine-tuning after every pruning step.

title: Replacement Learning: Training Neural Networks with Fewer Parameters
summary: End-to-end training with full-depth backpropagation remains the dominant paradigm for optimizing deep neural networks, but its efficiency deteriorates as models grow deeper. Since every block must be executed and differentiated under a single global objective, full-depth BP introduces substantial parameter redundancy, activation-memory cost, and training latency, especially when neighboring layers exhibit highly correlated learning patterns. Directly skipping or removing layers can reduce cost, but often weakens representation capacity or requires architecture-specific reuse designs. In this paper, we propose Replacement Learning (RepL), a training-time paradigm that reduces full-depth redundancy by replacing selected blocks rather than simply discarding them. For each removed block, RepL inserts a lightweight computing layer that synthesizes a surrogate operator from the parameters of its adjacent preceding and succeeding blocks through a learnable transformation, and applies the synthesized operator to the preceding activation. In this way, RepL preserves local contextual continuity while avoiding unnecessary full-layer computation. We instantiate RepL for CNNs and ViTs with tailored parameter-fusion blocks that handle convolutional channels, feature resolutions, and transformer submodules. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10, SVHN, STL-10, ImageNet, COCO, and CityScapes show that RepL reduces trainable parameters, GPU memory usage, and training time while matching or surpassing standard end-to-end training across classification, detection, and segmentation. Additional results on WikiText-2, transfer learning, inference throughput, checkpointing, stochastic depth, and INT8 quantization further demonstrate its generality and compatibility.

AI.tldr: Replacement Learning (RepL) reduces training overhead in deep neural networks by replacing selected blocks with lightweight synthesized operators rather than simply removing them, preserving representation capacity while cutting parameters, memory, and training time across multiple tasks and benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Full-depth backpropagation suffers from parameter redundancy, high activation-memory cost, and training latency as models grow deeper, with neighboring layers often learning correlated patterns inefficiently.
AI.method: RepL replaces selected blocks with lightweight computing layers that synthesize surrogate operators from adjacent preceding and succeeding blocks via a learnable transformation, applied to the preceding activation to preserve local continuity while avoiding full-layer computation.
AI.result: Experiments on CIFAR-10, SVHN, STL-10, ImageNet, COCO, CityScapes show reduced parameters, GPU memory, and training time while matching/surpassing standard training across classification, detection, segmentation tasks; also validated on WikiText-2, transfer learning, inference throughput, quantization, etc.
AI.conclusion: RepL offers a general and compatible training paradigm that reduces full-depth redundancy more effectively than layer skipping, with broad applicability across architectures (CNNs, ViTs) and tasks.

title: Towards Family-Grouped Hierarchical Federated Learning on Sub-5KB Models: A Feasibility Study of Privacy-Preserving ECG Monitoring for Ultra-Resource-Constrained Wearables
comment: Supported by Shenzhen Coddie Technology Co., Ltd. This is a preprint and has not been peer-reviewed
summary: Cardiovascular disease remains the leading cause of death worldwide, and early detection of arrhythmias through continuous ECG monitoring on wearable devices can prevent life-threatening events. Federated Learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative training by keeping raw ECG data on device, yet standard FL incurs prohibitive communication overhead and standard deep learning models cannot fit on ultra-low-power microcontrollers. We propose Family-Grouped Hierarchical Federated Learning (Family-FL), a three-tier architecture that uses the family as a natural privacy boundary for intra-family aggregation before global synchronization. We further design a hardware-constrained Tiny CNN-LSTM architecture with only 669 parameters, INT8-quantized to occupy merely 4.65KB Flash and 2.95KB RAM, meeting the constraints of STC32G12K128-class microcontrollers. Experiments on the MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database (mean of 5 independent runs with different seeds) demonstrate that Family-FL reduces communication volume by 76.7% compared to FedAvg while maintaining comparable accuracy. Family-FL-Tiny achieves 91.9 +/- 1.2% accuracy with macro-F1 of 0.483 +/- 0.031, reducing total communication to 0.31% of FedAvg. The model achieves reliable ventricular arrhythmia detection (per-class F1 = 0.80), the most clinically critical abnormality for home-based preliminary screening. These results demonstrate the technical feasibility of privacy-preserving federated learning on ultra-resource-constrained microcontrollers through simulation-based evaluation.
