The Aristotle output summary identifies the intended remaining mathematical step as the global counting step needed to show that these membership facts produce at least n distinct forbidden values, contradicting the cardinality assumption |M| < n; the Lean source itself does not reduce the main theorem to a separately encoded counting lemma. This case study gives an inspectable example of a central limitation in AI-assisted formalization, namely that local proof search can succeed while the global combinatorial bookkeeping required for a theorem remains unresolved. The paper contributes a reproducible Lean artifact and a precise analysis of its verified and unverified proof content.

AI.tldr: This paper reports a Lean 4 formalization case study of an Aristotle AI proof attempt for IMO 2009 Problem 6 (Grasshopper problem). Four helper lemmas were verified, but the main theorem remains open. The study illustrates that AI-assisted local proof search can succeed while global combinatorial bookkeeping remains unresolved.
AI.motivation: To examine the evidential status of AI-assisted theorem proving by analyzing a concrete case where local proof components succeed but the global theorem remains unproven, highlighting a central limitation of current AI formalization approaches.
AI.method: A Lean 4 formalization case study of an Aristotle API-generated proof attempt for the Grasshopper problem (IMO 2009 P6), analyzing which components were verified (four helper lemmas) versus unverified (main theorem left as sorry).
AI.result: The verified lemmas establish: (1) final partial sum equals total sum, (2) adjacent transposition affects only relevant intermediate partial sum, (3) changed partial sum has expected form, (4) maximality at a position admitting successor swap forces forbidden-set membership. The main theorem remains unresolved with a sorry, and the Lean source does not reduce it to a separate counting lemma.
AI.conclusion: This case study provides an inspectable example showing that AI-assisted formalization can succeed at local proof search while failing at the global combinatorial bookkeeping essential for completing the theorem, representing a central limitation of current AI-assisted theorem proving.

title: White-Balance First, Adjust Later: Cross-Camera Color Constancy via Vision-Language Evaluation
summary: Color constancy aims to keep object colors consistent under varying illumination. Cross-camera generalization in color constancy remains challenging because learning-based models often overfit to the color response characteristics of the training camera, resulting in degraded performance on images captured by other cameras. We propose VLM-CC, a feedback-guided framework that formulates color constancy as an iterative refinement process. Instead of directly estimating the illuminant from raw input, VLM-CC performs iterative correction driven by vision-language model (VLM)-based evaluation. At each iteration, the image is white-balanced using the current estimate and converted to pseudo-sRGB. A lightweight LoRA-tuned VLM then assesses the corrected image, identifying the dominant residual color cast and providing qualitative feedback. This feedback is mapped to a residual illumination direction (red, green, or blue) and used to update the illuminant estimate until convergence. Our key idea is to reframe color constancy as an iterative perceptual feedback problem, leveraging VLM evaluation instead of direct RGB regression. By replacing direct RGB estimation with VLM-guided perceptual feedback, VLM-CC achieves state-of-the-art robustness in cross-camera color constancy across multiple datasets. Code will be available at https://github.com/NothingIknow/VLM-CC.

AI.tldr: VLM-CC is a feedback-guided framework for cross-camera color constancy that iteratively refines white balance by using a vision-language model to evaluate residual color casts, achieving state-of-the-art cross-camera robustness.
AI.motivation: Learning-based color constancy models overfit to specific camera color response characteristics, leading to degraded performance on images from unseen cameras. Cross-camera generalization is a key unsolved challenge.
AI.method: VLM-CC formulates color constancy as an iterative refinement loop: at each iteration, the image is white-balanced and converted to pseudo-sRGB, a LoRA-tuned VLM evaluates the image to identify residual color cast, and the feedback is mapped to an RGB residual direction to update the illuminant estimate until convergence.
AI.result: VLM-CC achieves state-of-the-art robustness in cross-camera color constancy across multiple datasets, outperforming prior direct regression-based methods.
AI.conclusion: Reframing color constancy as an iterative perceptual feedback problem using VLM evaluation instead of direct RGB regression significantly improves cross-camera generalization.

title: Emergence of a Flow-Assisted Casting Strategy for Olfactory Navigation via Memory-Augmented Reinforcement Learning
summary: In dynamic flow fields, various animals exhibit remarkable odor search capabilities despite relying on stochastic detections. Interestingly, there exists an optimal time window for integrating these detections that maximizes search efficiency. To understand the underlying mechanism, we investigate the navigation performance of Reinforcement Learning (RL) agents in unsteady flows under varying memory lengths and flow conditions. Without any predefined models, the agents develop a flow-assisted casting strategy and adaptively adjust both the geometry of their search trajectories and the concentration threshold for initiating casting to maximize the success rate. The agent's average speed toward the odor source exhibits a non-monotonic dependence on memory length, which can be explained by the "sector-search" model.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how memory length affects odor source localization in dynamic flows, finding an optimal integration time window that maximizes search efficiency through RL-trained agents that develop adaptive casting strategies.
AI.motivation: To understand the mechanism behind the optimal time window for integrating stochastic odor detections in dynamic flow fields, which animals naturally exhibit for efficient odor search.
AI.method: Reinforcement Learning agents trained in unsteady flows with varying memory lengths and flow conditions, without predefined models, to study navigation performance.
AI.result: Agents develop a flow-assisted casting strategy with adaptive trajectory geometry and concentration thresholds; average speed toward the source shows a non-monotonic dependence on memory length, explained by the "sector-search" model.
AI.conclusion: There is an optimal memory length (time window) for odor source localization, balancing between having enough information and avoiding outdated detections in dynamic flows.

title: Not Every Rubric Teaches Equally: Policy-Aware Rubric Rewards for RLVR
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards has made post-training highly effective when correctness can be checked automatically. However, many important model behaviors require satisfying several qualitative criteria at once. Rubric-based rewards address this setting by grading prompt-specific criteria and aggregating them into a scalar reward. Yet standard static aggregations conflate a criterion's human-assigned importance with its current usefulness as an optimization signal. We show that this assumption breaks down in rubric RL: many important criteria are already saturated or currently unreachable, while criteria that distinguish rollouts are not necessarily those with the largest human weights. We introduce POW3R, a policy-aware rubric reward framework that preserves human weights and category balance as the rubric objective while adapting criterion-level reward weights during training. POW3R uses rollout-level contrast to emphasize criteria that currently separate the policy's outputs, making the GRPO reward more informative without changing the underlying evaluation target. Across three base policies on two datasets spanning multimodal and text-only settings, POW3R wins $24$ of $30$ base-policy/metric comparisons, improving both mean rubric reward and strict completion (the fraction of prompts whose response satisfies every required rubric criterion) over vanilla GRPO with rubric rewards, and reaches the same plateau in $2.5$--$4\times$ fewer training steps. Rubric rewards should therefore distinguish what should matter in the final answer from what can teach the current policy.

AI.tldr: POW3R is a policy-aware rubric reward framework that dynamically adjusts criterion-level weights during RL training, avoiding the pitfalls of static rubric aggregation that conflates human importance with optimization utility. It improves both reward quality and training efficiency across multiple settings.
AI.motivation: Standard static rubric reward aggregation conflates a criterion's human-assigned importance with its current usefulness as an optimization signal. Important criteria may be saturated or unreachable, while distinguishing criteria may not have the largest human weights, making static aggregation suboptimal for RL.
AI.method: POW3R (Policy-aware Optimized Weighted Rubric Rewards) dynamically adapts criterion-level reward weights during training using rollout-level contrast, emphasizing criteria that currently separate the policy's outputs, while preserving human weights and category balance as the rubric objective.
AI.result: Across three base policies on two datasets (multimodal and text-only), POW3R wins 24 of 30 base-policy/metric comparisons, improves both mean rubric reward and strict completion rate, and reaches the same plateau in 2.5-4x fewer training steps compared to vanilla GRPO with static rubric rewards.
AI.conclusion: Rubric rewards should distinguish what should matter in the final answer from what can teach the current policy, and dynamic weighting based on policy-aware signals is more effective than static human-assigned importance weights.

title: Bézier Degradation Modeling for LiDAR-based Human Motion Capture
summary: LiDAR-based 3D human motion capture has broad applications in fields such as autonomous driving and robotics, where accurate motion reconstruction is crucial. However, existing methods often struggle with unstable inputs and severe occlusions, leading to jittery or even failed pose predictions. To address these challenges, we propose BMLiCap, a coarse-to-fine framework that models motion using temporally compressible Bézier curves. By reducing control points through a trajectory-preserving strategy, we obtain a coherent and learning-friendly motion representation. To reconstruct human actions from LiDAR point-cloud cues, we design a progressive motion-reconstruction module. Specifically, a Time-scale Motion Transformer (TMT) is introduced to predict motion curves at multiple temporal scales, and a Multi-level Motion Aggregator (MMA) is utilized to adaptively fuse the multi-scale curves to recover detailed, temporally coherent poses, effectively bridging observation gaps caused by occlusions and noise. Across four mainstream benchmarks LiDARHuman26M, FreeMotion, NoiseMotion, and SLOPER4D, BMLiCap achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and temporal continuity in complex scenes, demonstrating its ability to compensate for severe occlusions and reduce prediction jitter.

AI.tldr: BMLiCap is a coarse-to-fine framework for LiDAR-based 3D human motion capture that uses temporally compressible Bézier curves, a Time-scale Motion Transformer (TMT), and a Multi-level Motion Aggregator (MMA) to achieve state-of-the-art accuracy and temporal continuity under occlusions and noise.
AI.motivation: Existing LiDAR-based 3D human motion capture methods struggle with unstable inputs and severe occlusions, resulting in jittery or failed pose predictions.
AI.method: BMLiCap employs a coarse-to-fine framework using temporally compressible Bézier curves with trajectory-preserving control point reduction. It features a Time-scale Motion Transformer (TMT) for multi-temporal-scale curve prediction and a Multi-level Motion Aggregator (MMA) for adaptive fusion of curves to recover detailed, temporally coherent poses.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and temporal continuity on four benchmarks: LiDARHuman26M, FreeMotion, NoiseMotion, and SLOPER4D, especially under severe occlusions and noise.
AI.conclusion: BMLiCap effectively compensates for occlusions, reduces prediction jitter, and sets a new state-of-the-art for LiDAR-based 3D human motion capture in complex scenes.

title: To Call or Not to Call: Diagnosing Intrinsic Over-Calling Bias in LLM Agents
summary: LLM agents exhibit a consistent tendency to over-call, invoking tools even in situations where none is needed. On the When2Call benchmark, six models from three families show high call accuracy but much lower no-call accuracy, leaving overall accuracy in the 55%-70% range. We trace this to an Intrinsic Bias Hypothesis (IBH): the call/no-call decision mapping carries an activation-independent call offset, so the model favors call even at activation parity. Using Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs), we recover behavior-aligned feature bases for the call/no_call decision, reduce them to a signed activation margin, and estimate the offset directly. Across all six models, the model is decision-neutral only when no_call activation outweighs call activation, consistent with IBH. We then causally test IBH with Adaptive Margin-Calibrated Steering (AMCS), a closed-form counter-bias shift along SAE decoder directions. Cancelling the diagnosed offset mitigates over-calling and improves overall accuracy with a negligible drop in call accuracy. Our work recasts over-calling from an empirical phenomenon into a mechanistic object amenable to causal correction. Code is available at https://github.com/SKURA502/agent-sae/.

AI.tldr: LLM agents show a systematic "over-calling" bias (using tools when unnecessary). The paper identifies an intrinsic bias toward calling via Sparse Autoencoders, quantifies it, and proposes Adaptive Margin-Calibrated Steering (AMCS) to correct it, improving overall accuracy from 55-70% range.
AI.motivation: LLM agents consistently over-call tools even when no tool is needed, degrading overall accuracy. The authors aim to understand and mechanistically correct this systematic failure.
AI.method: 1) Use Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) to recover feature bases for call/no-call decisions. 2) Reduce decisions to a signed activation margin and estimate an intrinsic call offset (Intrinsic Bias Hypothesis). 3) Propose Adaptive Margin-Calibrated Steering (AMCS) to apply a counter-bias shift along SAE decoder directions.
AI.result: All six tested models exhibit the predicted intrinsic call bias (decision-neutral only when no-call activation > call activation). AMCS mitigates over-calling, improving overall accuracy with negligible drop in call accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Over-calling is transformed from an empirical observation into a mechanistic, causally correctable bias.

title: HaorFloodAlert: Deseasonalized ML Ensemble for 72-Hour Flood Prediction in Bangladesh Haor Wetlands
comment: 9 pages, 9 figures. To be submitted to raaicon.org
summary:
Flash floods in Bangladesh's haor wetlands show up with almost no warning. They wreck the annual boro rice harvest. Current setups, built for riverine floods, miss backwater dynamics entirely. These basins are flat. Water does not behave like it does on the Brahmaputra.
  We built HaorFloodAlert, a deseasonalized machine learning ensemble that forecasts 72-hour flood probability for the Sunamganj Haor (approximately 8,000 km2). Temperature was acting as a seasonal cheat code - it inflated accuracy by 6.9 pp just because floods happen in warm months. We caught that. We also built an upstream Barak River Sentinel-1 SAR proxy from Silchar, Assam, giving about 36 hours of lead time. Otsu-thresholded SAR change detection validates at 84-91 percent spatial match.
  The operational ensemble (RF 0.5625 + XGBoost 0.4375) hits 89.6 percent LOOCV accuracy, 87.5 percent recall, and 0.943 AUC-ROC on 77 real Sentinel-1 events. A three-tier alert pipeline and a BRRI-calibrated boro rice damage estimator are included.

AI.tldr: A 72-hour flood forecasting system for Bangladesh's haor wetlands using a deseasonalized ML ensemble and upstream SAR data, achieving 89.6% accuracy with real-time alerts and rice damage estimation.
AI.motivation: Flash floods in Bangladesh's haor wetlands occur with minimal warning, devastating boro rice harvests. Existing flood prediction systems designed for riverine floods fail to capture backwater dynamics in these flat basins.
AI.method: HaorFloodAlert uses a deseasonalized machine learning ensemble (RF 0.5625 + XGBoost 0.4375) with temperature bias correction and an upstream Barak River Sentinel-1 SAR proxy from Silchar, Assam providing ~36 hours lead time. Otsu-thresholded SAR change detection validates at 84-91% spatial match.
AI.result: 89.6% LOOCV accuracy, 87.5% recall, 0.943 AUC-ROC on 77 real Sentinel-1 events. Temperature bias inflated accuracy by 6.9 pp (detected and corrected). Includes a three-tier alert pipeline and BRRI-calibrated boro rice damage estimator.
AI.conclusion: The system demonstrates effective operational flood forecasting specifically for haor wetlands, with careful deseasonalization preventing false accuracy inflation from temperature-season correlations.

title: UniRefiner: Teaching Pre-trained ViTs to Self-Dispose Dross via Contrastive Register
summary: Representation learning with Vision Transformers (ViTs) has advanced rapidly, yet the utility of large-scale models in spatially sensitive tasks is hindered by spurious tokens. Prior efforts to mitigate this have been limited, often defining these artifacts narrowly, for example, as simple high-norm outliers. We argue that this scope is insufficient. For dense prediction tasks, we posit that any token failing to encode location-aligned semantics should be treated as a spurious artifact. This broader definition reveals a more complex problem, leading us to systematically categorize and characterize three fundamental types of spurious tokens that corrupt spatial representations. Based on this comprehensive diagnosis, we propose UniRefiner, a universal refinement framework that teaches pre-trained ViTs to self-dispose of these artifacts. UniRefiner uses contrastive registers to explicitly isolate and redistribute spurious tokens via a dual objective: (i) it aligns image tokens with filtered regular tokens to preserve semantics, and (ii) it aligns register tokens with detected spurious tokens to capture the spurious signals. Our method requires only a few epochs of fine-tuning on ~5k images to refine diverse ViTs, including massive models like EVA-CLIP-8B and InternViT-6B. Experiments demonstrate consistent and significant improvements: notably, the refined EVA-CLIP-8B achieves 51.9\% mIoU on ADE20K (+9.4\%), surpassing specialized vision models like DINOv2 (49.1\%), while zero-shot segmentation accuracy improves by up to 22\%. UniRefiner unlocks the latent spatial potential of existing large-scale foundation models, paving the way for their broader application.

AI.tldr: UniRefiner is a universal refinement framework that identifies and removes three types of spurious tokens in Vision Transformers, improving dense prediction tasks. It uses contrastive registers and fine-tunes on ~5k images to boost segmentation performance (e.g., +9.4% mIoU on ADE20K for EVA-CLIP-8B).
AI.motivation: Existing definitions of spurious tokens in ViTs are too narrow (e.g., only high-norm outliers). For dense prediction tasks, any token failing to encode location-aligned semantics should be considered spurious, but prior work lacks a comprehensive solution.
AI.method: UniRefiner uses contrastive registers with a dual objective: (1) align image tokens with filtered regular tokens to preserve semantics, and (2) align register tokens with detected spurious tokens to capture spurious signals. Requires only a few epochs of fine-tuning on ~5k images.
AI.result: EVA-CLIP-8B refined achieves 51.9% mIoU on ADE20K (+9.4%), surpassing DINOv2 (49.1%). Zero-shot segmentation accuracy improves by up to 22%. Works across diverse ViTs including massive models like InternViT-6B.
AI.conclusion: UniRefiner unlocks the latent spatial potential of large-scale foundation models, enabling broader application of ViTs in spatially sensitive tasks through a simple, universal refinement approach.

title: Prediction Is Not Physics: Learning and Evaluating Conserved Quantities in Neural Simulators
summary: A diffusion model trained on Hamiltonian trajectories can achieve rollout MSE near $10^{-3}$, but the standard deviation of its energy over time is between 7500 and 36000 times larger than the ground-truth energy standard deviation, indicating a failure to preserve conservation laws. This gap motivates our central question of whether neural networks can learn or select globally conserved quantities from physical trajectories. We investigate this across three Hamiltonian systems: projectile motion, pendulum, and spring-mass. We use a structured $T(v)+V(q)$ energy model, a black-box Conservation Discovery Network (CDN), a polynomial CDN, and a conditional diffusion baseline. The structured network reaches $R^2 \geq 0.9999$ against analytical energy on clean data, while the black-box CDN reaches $R^2 \geq 0.996$ when trained with temporal consistency plus a small alignment loss to analytical energy at $t=0$ ($λ_{\mathrm{align}}=0.2$). With $λ_{\mathrm{align}}=0$, CDN Pearson $R^2$ collapses on pendulum and spring-mass ($< 10^{-3}$), showing that temporal consistency alone is not enough to reliably identify the true energy. Under $1\%$ additive Gaussian noise, the CDN outperforms the structured model on the projectile and spring-mass systems, suggesting that the CDN may be more robust to noisy inputs in this setting. However, the polynomial CDN is sensitive to training configuration: it achieves $R^2=0.78$ under a short training schedule on the pendulum system, but reaches $R^2=0.9998$ with more training time and data, regardless of whether noise is added.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether neural networks can learn conserved quantities (like energy) from physical trajectories, finding that structured energy models perform well on clean data while black-box Conservation Discovery Networks (CDNs) require some alignment signal to identify true energy, and CDNs show better robustness to noise than structured models.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models trained on Hamiltonian trajectories fail to preserve conservation laws (energy variance 7500-36000x larger than ground truth), motivating the question of whether neural networks can learn globally conserved quantities from physical trajectories.
AI.method: The authors evaluate four approaches across projectile motion, pendulum, and spring-mass systems: (1) a structured T(v)+V(q) energy model, (2) a black-box Conservation Discovery Network (CDN), (3) a polynomial CDN, and (4) a conditional diffusion baseline. They test temporal consistency, alignment loss, and robustness to Gaussian noise.
AI.result: Structured network achieves R² ≥ 0.9999 against analytical energy on clean data. Black-box CDN reaches R² ≥ 0.996 with temporal consistency + small alignment loss (λ=0.2), but collapses to <10⁻³ without alignment. Under 1% noise, CDN outperforms structured model on projectile and spring-mass. Polynomial CDN is sensitive to training configuration, ranging from R²=0.78 to 0.9998.
AI.conclusion: Temporal consistency alone is insufficient to reliably identify true energy; some alignment signal is needed. Black-box CDNs show potential for robustness to noisy inputs compared to structured models, while polynomial CDNs require careful training configuration.

title: A Methodology for Selecting and Composing Runtime Architecture Patterns for Production LLM Agents
comment: 25 pages, 2 figures, 6 tables. Companion repo at https://github.com/vasundras/agent-runtime-patterns
summary:
Production LLM agents combine stochastic model outputs with deterministic software systems, yet the boundary between the two is rarely treated as a first-class architectural object. This paper names that boundary the stochastic-deterministic boundary (SDB): a four-part contract among a proposer, verifier, commit step, and reject signal that specifies how an LLM output becomes a system action. We argue that the SDB is the load-bearing primitive of production agent runtimes.
  Around this primitive, we organize agent runtime design into three concerns: Coordination, State, and Control. We present a catalog of six runtime patterns that compose the SDB differently across conversational, autonomous, and long-horizon agents: hierarchical delegation, scatter-gather plus saga, event-driven sequencing, shared state machine, supervisor plus gate, and human in the loop. For each pattern, we trace its lineage to distributed-systems concepts and identify what changes when the worker is stochastic.
  The paper contributes a five-step methodology for selecting runtime patterns, a diagnostic procedure that maps production failures to pattern weaknesses, and a failure mode called replay divergence, in which LLM-based consumers of a deterministic event log produce different downstream outputs under model-version or prompt changes. A stylized reliability decomposition separates per-call model variance from architectural momentum, motivating the claim that as model variance decreases, pattern choice and SDB strength become increasingly important levers for long-run reliability. We apply the methodology to five workloads and provide one runnable reference implementation for a 90-day contract-renewal agent.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the stochastic-deterministic boundary (SDB) as a first-class architectural primitive for production LLM agents, presents six runtime patterns for composing SDBs across different agent types, and offers a methodology for selecting patterns and diagnosing failures.
AI.motivation: The boundary between stochastic LLM outputs and deterministic software systems in production agents is rarely treated as a first-class architectural object, leading to ad-hoc designs and reliability issues.
AI.method: The authors name the stochastic-deterministic boundary (SDB) as a four-part contract (proposer, verifier, commit step, reject signal), organize runtime design into three concerns (Coordination, State, Control), present six runtime patterns, and develop a five-step selection methodology plus failure diagnosis procedure.
AI.result: Catalog of six runtime patterns (hierarchical delegation, scatter-gather plus saga, event-driven sequencing, shared state machine, supervisor plus gate, human in the loop); identification of replay divergence as a failure mode; a reliability decomposition separating per-call variance from architectural momentum.
AI.conclusion: As model variance decreases over time, pattern choice and SDB strength become increasingly important levers for long-run reliability of production LLM agents.

title: PrAda: Few-Shot Visual Adaptation for Text-Prompted Segmentation
summary: Segmenting images is critical for visual understanding but demands extensive pixel-level annotations. Foundational models have enabled new paradigms for predicting new classes guided by textual prompts, without annotations from the target domain. Yet, on specialized target domains, far from the original pre-training, their performance degrades. We study the errors of existing methods under such domain-shift, finding that misclassification rather than mask generation is the main culprit. To address this, we introduce the novel problem of Few-Shot Visual Adaptation for text-prompted Segmentation. This kind of adaptation has been largely studied for image classification, but it remains unexplored for segmentation. We tackle this task with Prototype Adaptation (PrAda), a novel, parameter-efficient method that adapts a frozen text-prompted segmentation model. Our approach learns class-specific prototypes by combining fine-grained pixel features and high-level transformer representations, which are then fused with the original text-based predictions through a learned importance factor. This preserves the model's zero-shot potential while enabling strong adaptation to new domains. Experiments across semantic, instance, and panoptic segmentation on five benchmarks demonstrate that PrAda yields significant improvements over state-of-the-art and proposed baselines.

AI.tldr: PrAda introduces a parameter-efficient prototype adaptation method to improve text-prompted segmentation under domain shift, addressing misclassification errors by fusing pixel features with transformer representations while preserving zero-shot capabilities.
AI.motivation: Existing text-prompted segmentation models degrade significantly on specialized target domains due to domain shift, with misclassification (not mask generation) being the primary error source. The authors identify the need for few-shot adaptation tailored to segmentation, which remains unexplored.
AI.method: PrAda (Prototype Adaptation) adapts a frozen text-prompted segmentation model by learning class-specific prototypes that combine fine-grained pixel features and high-level transformer representations. These prototypes are fused with original text-based predictions via a learned importance factor, preserving zero-shot potential while enabling domain adaptation.
AI.result: Experiments across semantic, instance, and panoptic segmentation on five benchmarks show PrAda yields significant improvements over state-of-the-art and proposed baselines.
AI.conclusion: PrAda effectively addresses domain shift in text-prompted segmentation through few-shot visual adaptation, demonstrating strong performance gains while maintaining the model's original zero-shot generalization ability.

title: Navigating the Emotion Tree: Hierarchical Hyperbolic RAG for Multimodal Emotion Recognition
summary: Multimodal emotion recognition aims to integrate text, audio, and video sources to understand human affective states. Although multimodal large language models excel at multimodal reasoning, they typically treat emotion categories as independent labels, ignoring the rich hierarchical taxonomy of human psychology. Moreover, lacking external contextual knowledge makes them highly susceptible to over-interpreting noisy cues, further complicating fine-grained emotion classification. To address these issues, we propose \textbf{HyperEmo-RAG}, a retrieval-augmented generation framework that leverages a structured emotional knowledge base. Our framework introduces two key innovations. 1) Hierarchical hyperbolic grounding. Recognizing the inherent hierarchical tree structure of emotion taxonomies, we jointly embed hierarchical emotion labels and multimodal samples into a continuous hyperbolic space (Poincaré ball) and design a hierarchical beam-search deliberation process that progressively retrieves samples from coarse to fine-grained levels. 2) Structured evidence injection. Based on the retrieved evidence, we construct an evidence graph and inject the structured knowledge as explicit cognitive context into the LLM through a Tree-Aware Attention mechanism and an EmotionGraphFormer, preserving the integrity of graph-structured information. Experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate that HyperEmo-RAG significantly outperforms existing methods.

AI.tldr: HyperEmo-RAG is a retrieval-augmented generation framework that uses a hierarchical hyperbolic knowledge base and structured evidence graphs to improve multimodal emotion recognition by capturing the hierarchical taxonomy of emotions and reducing over-interpretation of noisy cues.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal emotion recognition methods treat emotion categories as independent labels, ignoring the inherent hierarchical taxonomy of human psychology. Additionally, they lack external contextual knowledge, making them prone to over-interpreting noisy cues in fine-grained emotion classification.
AI.method: The framework introduces two innovations: 1) Hierarchical hyperbolic grounding - embedding emotion labels and multimodal samples into Poincaré ball space for coarse-to-fine retrieval via hierarchical beam-search. 2) Structured evidence injection - constructing evidence graphs from retrieved samples and injecting them into LLMs via Tree-Aware Attention and EmotionGraphFormer.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple datasets show HyperEmo-RAG significantly outperforms existing methods in multimodal emotion recognition.
AI.conclusion: HyperEmo-RAG effectively addresses the limitations of prior work by leveraging hierarchical emotion taxonomies and structured knowledge injection, leading to superior fine-grained emotion classification performance.

title: Component-Aware Structure-Preserving Style Transfer for Satellite Sim2Real 6D Pose Estimation
summary: Monocular 6D pose estimation for non-cooperative satellites depends heavily on annotated training data, yet real satellite images with reliable pose labels and component-level masks are difficult to acquire at scale. Synthetic rendering can provide exact geometric annotations, but the appearance gap between rendered and real observations limits direct transfer to the real domain. This paper presents a component-aware structure-preserving style transfer framework for satellite synthetic-to-real data construction. The method builds weakly paired real--synthetic samples from calibrated real acquisition, ArUco-based camera-pose measurement, CAD rendering, and component masks. It then extracts part-wise real-domain style codes from unlabeled real images and injects them into corresponding synthetic satellite regions through mask-aligned modulation. To keep the generated images usable for downstream supervision, adversarial training is combined with local contrastive consistency, self-regularization, and edge-preserving constraints. Experiments are conducted on 5,000 rendered satellite images and 100 real images captured in a calibrated setup. The real images provide target-domain appearance references and final evaluation images, while the downstream GDRNet pose estimator is trained only on synthetic or translated synthetic images. Compared with representative image-translation baselines, the proposed method achieves the lowest image distribution discrepancy, with an FID of 54.32 and a KID of 0.048. When the translated data are used to train GDRNet in this target-domain adaptation setting, the ADD pass rate improves to 0.260 and the AUC improves to 0.611. These results indicate that component-level appearance transfer can improve satellite Sim2Real pose estimation in the considered calibrated setup while retaining simulation-derived geometric annotations.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a component-aware style transfer framework that translates synthetic satellite images to realistic ones by injecting part-wise real-domain style codes, enabling improved monocular 6D pose estimation for non-cooperative satellites without requiring large-scale real annotated data.
AI.motivation: Monocular 6D pose estimation for non-cooperative satellites requires annotated training data, but real satellite images with reliable pose labels and component-level masks are difficult to acquire at scale. Synthetic data is available but suffers from an appearance gap when transferred to the real domain.
AI.method: A component-aware structure-preserving style transfer framework using weakly paired real–synthetic samples, part-wise real-domain style codes extracted from unlabeled real images, mask-aligned modulation, adversarial training, local contrastive consistency, self-regularization, and edge-preserving constraints.
AI.result: The method achieves an FID of 54.32 and KID of 0.048. For downstream GDRNet pose estimation, ADD pass rate improves to 0.260 and AUC to 0.611, outperforming representative image-translation baselines.
AI.conclusion: Component-level appearance transfer can improve satellite Sim2Real pose estimation in calibrated setups while preserving simulation-derived geometric annotations.

title: Soft Learning
summary: Modern machine learning forces practitioners to choose between powerful but expensive deep networks and fast but limited classical algorithms. Here we introduce Soft Learning, a framework that maintains a library of heterogeneous specialists -- spanning linear models, tree ensembles, kernel machines, and neural networks -- and discovers provably optimal combination weights through cross-validated non-negative least squares. Soft Learning is guaranteed to match or exceed the best weighted combination of its specialists, trains over two orders of magnitude faster than deep networks on CPU alone (72-435x faster across tested configurations), provides inherent interpretability through learned weights that reveal which algorithmic paradigm best fits the data, and is future-proof: adding specialists is mathematically guaranteed to maintain or improve performance. Across 37 datasets (25 classification, 12 regression) against nine methods including CatBoost and tuned deep networks, Soft Learning ranks first on 70% of tasks, achieves the best mean rank (Friedman test, p = 1.12 x 10^-12), and is the only method to simultaneously excel at both classification and regression -- all without GPU hardware or hyperparameter tuning. These results suggest a paradigm shift from "which algorithm is best?" to "what is the provably optimal combination?" -- a question Soft Learning answers with formal guarantees for any data modality.

AI.tldr: Soft Learning combines heterogeneous ML specialists via cross-validated non-negative least squares, provably matching the best weighted combination, training 72-435x faster than deep networks on CPU, and ranking first on 70% of tasks across 37 datasets without GPU or hyperparameter tuning.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between powerful but expensive deep networks and fast but limited classical algorithms, offering a provably optimal combination framework that avoids the traditional "which algorithm is best?" dilemma.
AI.method: Maintains a library of diverse specialists (linear models, tree ensembles, kernel machines, neural networks) and learns provably optimal combination weights via cross-validated non-negative least squares.
AI.result: 72-435x faster training than deep networks on CPU; ranks first on 70% of 37 datasets; best mean rank (Friedman test, p=1.12e-12); excels at both classification and regression without GPU or hyperparameter tuning.
AI.conclusion: Shifts the paradigm from selecting a single best algorithm to finding the provably optimal combination, with formal guarantees applicable to any data modality.

title: Robust Basis Spline Decoupling for the Compression of Transformer Models
summary: Decoupling is a powerful modeling paradigm for representing multivariate functions as compositions of linear transformations and univariate nonlinear functions. A single-layer decoupling can be viewed as a fully connected neural network with a single hidden layer and flexible activation functions, providing a direct link with neural networks. Because of this, the use of decoupling methods has gained increasing attention in neural network domains, particularly compression, since it enables structured approximations with reduced parameter complexity. Existing tensor-based decoupling methods typically rely on polynomial or piecewise-linear parameterizations of the internal nonlinear functions, which can suffer from numerical instability or limited expressiveness. In this work, we introduce a B-spline-based decoupling framework that generalizes these existing approaches. By exploiting the local support and flexible smoothness control of B-splines, the proposed formulation yields a more numerically stable and expressive representation. We derive a constrained coupled matrix-tensor factorization and propose a robust alternating least-squares algorithm, called R-CMTF-BSD, incorporating normalization and Tikhonov regularization. The proposed method is validated through experiments on synthetic data and transformer model compression. Results on the Vision and Swin Transformer architectures demonstrate that B-spline decoupling enables substantial parameter reduction while maintaining competitive accuracy, making the R-CMTF-BSD algorithm a promising tool for structured neural network compression.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a B-spline-based decoupling framework for neural network compression, which generalizes existing tensor-based decoupling methods, improves numerical stability and expressiveness, and demonstrates effective parameter reduction on Vision and Swin Transformers.
AI.motivation: Existing tensor-based decoupling methods rely on polynomial or piecewise-linear parameterizations that suffer from numerical instability or limited expressiveness, motivating the need for a more stable and flexible approach.
AI.method: The authors propose a B-spline-based decoupling framework with a constrained coupled matrix-tensor factorization and a robust alternating least-squares algorithm (R-CMTF-BSD) incorporating normalization and Tikhonov regularization.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic data and transformer model compression show that B-spline decoupling achieves substantial parameter reduction while maintaining competitive accuracy on Vision and Swin Transformer architectures.
AI.conclusion: The R-CMTF-BSD algorithm is a promising tool for structured neural network compression, offering a numerically stable and expressive alternative to existing decoupling methods.

title: P2DNav: Panorama-to-Downview Reasoning for Zero-shot Vision-and-Language Navigation
summary: Vision-and-language navigation (VLN) requires an embodied agent to ground natural-language instructions into executable navigation actions in unseen environments. Existing zero-shot methods typically rely on additional waypoint prediction modules, which often entangle high-level directional reasoning with fine-grained local grounding, leading to error-prone and unstable decisions. In this paper, we propose P2DNav, a hierarchical framework for zero-shot vision-and-language navigation. P2DNav consists of three core components: Panorama-to-Downview (P2D), Sliding-Window Dialogue Memory (SDM), and Reflective Reorientation Mechanism (RRM). P2D explicitly decomposes navigation decision-making into two stages: panoramic direction selection and downview local grounding. It first selects the instruction-relevant direction from a 360° panorama, and then predicts a pixel-level target point from the downview RGB observation in that direction. In addition, SDM organizes navigation history as a multi-turn dialogue context and maintains recent visual observations within a sliding window to support long-horizon navigation. RRM further enables reflective reorientation by assessing the reliability of local grounding based on the downview observation and returning to panoramic direction selection when necessary. Experiments on the R2R-CE benchmark show that P2DNav achieves strong performance among zero-shot methods. In particular, compared with the state-of-the-art (SOTA) zero-shot waypoint-based and waypoint-free methods, P2DNav achieves SR gains of 146.6% and 58.9%, respectively, demonstrating the effectiveness of P2D, SDM, and RRM for zero-shot VLN. Code will be released for public use.

AI.tldr: P2DNav is a hierarchical zero-shot VLN framework that decomposes navigation into panoramic direction selection and downview local grounding, using Sliding-Window Dialogue Memory and Reflective Reorientation to achieve strong performance on R2R-CE, outperforming SOTA zero-shot methods by 146.6% and 58.9% in SR.
AI.motivation: Existing zero-shot VLN methods rely on additional waypoint prediction modules that entangle high-level directional reasoning with fine-grained local grounding, leading to error-prone and unstable decisions.
AI.method: P2DNav is a hierarchical framework with three components: (1) Panorama-to-Downview (P2D) that decomposes decision-making into panoramic direction selection followed by pixel-level target prediction from downview RGB; (2) Sliding-Window Dialogue Memory (SDM) that organizes navigation history as multi-turn dialogue with recent visual observations; (3) Reflective Reorientation Mechanism (RRM) that assesses local grounding reliability and returns to panoramic selection when needed.
AI.result: On R2R-CE benchmark, P2DNav achieves strong zero-shot performance, with SR gains of 146.6% over SOTA zero-shot waypoint-based methods and 58.9% over waypoint-free methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed P2D, SDM, and RRM components are effective for zero-shot VLN, demonstrating that explicitly decoupling directional reasoning from local grounding significantly improves navigation performance.

title: Auditing Reasoning-Trace Memorization Claims after Unlearning with Head-Conditioned Canaries
summary: Evaluations of unlearning on reasoning models sometimes show a bypass pattern. The answer side looks unlearned, but the model's own thinking trace keeps emitting the forgotten content, and the gap is taken as evidence that the weights still remember. We audit this reading on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B with LoRA-memorized fictional authors and NPO unlearning, conditioned on a six-token canary head. On one seed, swapping the thinking trace for a short non-canary prefill on the same weights drops the answer rate by as much as the bypass gap itself, whether the prefill mimics the training template or not. On a second seed the bypass gap shrinks rather than vanishing, and the prefill swap reverses direction and brings the answer rate to ceiling. A positive parser-split bypass gap thus does not by itself identify hidden weight-level memorization, and does not rule it out either. On a different distillate the same metric flips sign because the parser cannot find the closing tag. We recommend a decode-time template swap as a cheap sanity check alongside the canonical audit.

AI.tldr: This paper audits bypass patterns in reasoning model unlearning, finding that a positive bypass gap (where thinking traces leak forgotten content but answers don't) does not reliably indicate hidden weight-level memorization, as template swaps can significantly affect answer rates, and recommends decode-time template swaps as a sanity check.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether the "bypass pattern" observed in unlearned reasoning models—where thinking traces emit forgotten content while answers appear unlearned—actually indicates persistent weight-level memorization, or if it can be an artifact of evaluation methodology.
AI.method: Audited DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B with LoRA-memorized fictional authors and NPO unlearning, conditioned on a six-token canary head. Tested by swapping the thinking trace for a short non-canary prefill on the same weights and measuring answer rate changes.
AI.result: On one seed, swapping thinking trace for non-canary prefill dropped answer rate as much as the bypass gap itself. On a second seed, the bypass gap shrank and the prefill swap reversed direction. On a different distillate, the metric flipped sign due to parser limitations (couldn't find closing tag).
AI.conclusion: A positive parser-split bypass gap does not reliably identify hidden weight-level memorization nor rule it out; recommends decode-time template swap as a cheap sanity check alongside canonical audit.

title: Benchmarking and Evolving Reason-Reflect-Rectify for Reflective Visual Generation
summary: Text-to-Image (T2I) models and Unified Multimodal Models (UMMs) have achieved remarkable progress in visual generation. However, their reliance on a single-pass generation paradigm limits their ability to handle complex prompts requiring iterative refinement. To enable multi-round Reflective Visual Generation (RVG), we formalize the Reason-Reflect-Rectify (R^3) loop as a core framework and introduce R^3-Bench, a benchmark of over 600 expert-annotated instances that quantifies iterative reasoning and rectification capabilities. Evaluation on R^3-Bench reveals a critical gap: while state-of-the-art models can identify generation errors, they fail to generate actionable rectification instructions. To bridge this gap, we propose R^3-Refiner, a dual-stage framework leveraging Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) and a Hierarchical Reward Mechanism (HRM) to better align rectification with reflective reasoning. Experiments show that R^3-Refiner achieves significant improvements on R^3-Bench (+12.0% in Reflective Verdict Score, +9.0% in Rectification Score), and can be seamlessly integrated with various MLLMs to enhance the generation quality of different T2I models on GenEval++ and T2I-CompBench. Code is available at https://github.com/xiaomoguhz/R3-Bench.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces R^3-Bench (600+ expert-annotated instances) for evaluating multi-round Reflective Visual Generation, reveals that current models fail to generate actionable rectification instructions, and proposes R^3-Refiner using GRPO and Hierarchical Reward Mechanism to achieve +12.0% Reflective Verdict Score and +9.0% Rectification Score improvements.
AI.motivation: Current T2I and UMMs use single-pass generation, which fails at handling complex prompts that require iterative refinement. The authors formalize the need for multi-round reflective visual generation.
AI.method: The paper proposes (1) R^3-Bench benchmark with 600+ expert-annotated instances, (2) R^3-Refiner framework using dual-stage training with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) and Hierarchical Reward Mechanism (HRM).
AI.result: R^3-Refiner achieves +12.0% in Reflective Verdict Score and +9.0% in Rectification Score on R^3-Bench, and improves generation quality on GenEval++ and T2I-CompBench.
AI.conclusion: R^3-Refiner effectively bridges the gap between error detection and actionable rectification in reflective visual generation, and is seamlessly integrable with various MLLMs and T2I models.

title: Data-Free Client Contribution Estimation via Logit Maximization for Federated Learning
summary: Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative learning of computer vision models, where privacy and regulatory constraints prevent centralizing data across devices or organizations. However, practical FL deployments often exhibit severe class imbalance and label skew, causing standard aggregation protocols to overfit dominant clients and degrade minority-class performance. We propose a data-free, class-wise contribution estimation and aggregation framework based on logit maximization (CELM) that does not require sharing raw data, client metadata, or auxiliary public datasets. The FL server probes client updates to obtain class-wise evidence scores and assembles a cross-client evidence matrix, which quantifies both per-class competence and class coverage. Using this matrix, we compute contribution weights that upweight clients providing strong, discriminative evidence for underrepresented classes. The resulting aggregation is stable due to simplex constraints and momentum smoothing, and it remains compatible with standard FL training pipelines. We evaluate the approach on representative vision benchmarks under controlled non-IID and pathological label splits, demonstrating that CELM-based aggregation improves robustness to imbalance and statistical heterogeneity, while yielding better performance without requiring any additional data exchange.

AI.tldr: Proposes CELM, a data-free class-wise contribution estimation and aggregation framework for federated learning that uses logit maximization to handle class imbalance and label skew without sharing raw data or auxiliary datasets.
AI.motivation: Practical FL deployments suffer from class imbalance and label skew across clients, causing standard aggregation protocols to overfit dominant clients and degrade minority-class performance.
AI.method: CELM framework: server probes client updates to obtain class-wise evidence scores, builds a cross-client evidence matrix, computes contribution weights via simplex constraints and momentum smoothing, and upweights clients with strong evidence for underrepresented classes.
AI.result: Evaluated on vision benchmarks under non-IID and pathological label splits; CELM-based aggregation improves robustness to imbalance and statistical heterogeneity with better performance and no additional data exchange.
AI.conclusion: CELM provides an effective, data-free solution for handling class imbalance in federated learning while remaining compatible with standard FL pipelines.

title: CAD-Free Learning of Spacecraft Pose Estimators via NeRF-Based Augmentations
summary: Spacecraft pose estimation networks require tens of thousands of CAD-rendered images to be trained. This reliance on synthetic CAD data (i) limits applicability to targets with reliable geometry prior, excluding uncooperative or poorly documented spacecraft, and (ii) causes poor generalization to real on-orbit conditions due to unrealistic illumination and material appearance. This paper introduces a NeRF-based image augmentation method that enables the learning of spacecraft pose estimators from only a few tens to a few hundreds of images. The method learns a Neural Radiance Field of the target and generates a large, diverse dataset through geometrically-consistent viewpoint and appearance augmentation. This augmented dataset enables the training of accurate target-specific pose estimators without requiring a CAD model or large synthetic datasets. Experiments show that our approach supports the training of accurate pose estimators from only 25 to 400 realistic images, even under severe illumination variations. When applied on large CAD-based synthetic datasets, the NeRF-based augmentation also enhances out-of-domain generalization, yielding improved robustness to real on-orbit conditions.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a NeRF-based augmentation method that trains spacecraft pose estimators using only tens to hundreds of real images, eliminating the need for CAD models or large synthetic datasets.
AI.motivation: Current spacecraft pose estimation relies on large CAD-rendered synthetic datasets, which (i) require reliable geometry prior unavailable for uncooperative/poorly documented spacecraft, and (ii) suffer from poor generalization to real on-orbit conditions due to unrealistic illumination and material appearance.
AI.method: The method learns a Neural Radiance Field (NeRF) of the target from a small number of real images, then generates a large, diverse augmented dataset through geometrically-consistent viewpoint and appearance augmentation. This augmented dataset is used to train target-specific pose estimators without CAD models.
AI.result: The approach supports training accurate pose estimators from only 25 to 400 realistic images under severe illumination variations. When applied to large CAD-based synthetic datasets, it also improves out-of-domain generalization and robustness to real on-orbit conditions.
AI.conclusion: NeRF-based augmentation enables effective spacecraft pose estimation with minimal real images, overcoming the limitations of CAD-dependent methods and improving real-world robustness.

title: Position: Graph Condensation Needs a Reset -- Move Beyond Full-dataset Training and Model-Dependence
summary:
Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are powerful tools for learning from graph-structured data, but their scalability is increasingly strained by the size of real-world graphs in domains like recommender systems, fraud detection, and molecular biology. Graph condensation -- the task of generating a smaller synthetic graph that retains the performance of models trained on the original -- has emerged as a promising solution. However, the dominant approach of gradient matching introduces a fundamental contradiction: it requires training on the full dataset to create the compressed version, thereby undermining the goal of efficiency. Worse still, these methods suffer from high computational overhead, poor generalization across GNN architectures, and brittle reliance on specific model configurations. Equally concerning is the community's reliance on misleading evaluation protocols such as node compression ratios, which fail to reflect true resource savings, condensation overhead, and illusory application to neural architecture search. These shortcomings are not incidental -- they are systemic, and they obstruct meaningful progress.
  In this position paper, we argue that graph condensation, in its current form, needs a reset. We call for moving beyond full-dataset training and model-dependent design, and instead advocate for methods that are lightweight, architecture-agnostic, and practically deployable. By identifying key methodological flaws and outlining concrete research directions, we aim to reorient the field toward approaches that deliver on the true promise of condensation: efficient, generalizable, and usable GNN training at scale.

AI.tldr: This position paper argues that current graph condensation methods for GNNs are fundamentally flawed due to reliance on full-dataset training, high computational cost, poor generalization, and misleading evaluation metrics, calling for a reset toward lightweight, architecture-agnostic, and practically deployable approaches.
AI.motivation: Graph condensation aims to generate smaller synthetic graphs that preserve model performance, addressing scalability issues of GNNs on large real-world graphs. However, existing gradient-matching methods require training on the full dataset (contradicting efficiency), suffer from high overhead, poor cross-architecture generalization, and rely on misleading evaluation protocols.
AI.method: This is a position paper (not introducing new methodology) that identifies systemic flaws in current graph condensation: (1) full-dataset training requirement, (2) model-dependent design, (3) misleading evaluation metrics like node compression ratios, and (4) illusory application to neural architecture search.
AI.result: No experimental results presented; instead, the paper provides a critical analysis of shortcomings in existing graph condensation literature and identifies systemic methodological and evaluation issues.
AI.conclusion: The authors call for a reset of graph condensation research, advocating for lightweight, architecture-agnostic methods with rigorous evaluation protocols that truly measure efficiency gains, moving beyond the current flawed paradigm.

title: Cross-View Splatter: Feed-Forward View Synthesis with Georeferenced Images
comment: Submitted to CVPR 2026. 8 figures, 3 tables. Project page: https://nianticspatial.github.io/cross-view-splatter/
summary: We present Cross-View Splatter, a feed-forward method that predicts pixel-aligned Gaussian splats for outdoor scenes captured at ground level AND by satellite. Faithful reconstructions require good camera coverage, but ground imagery is time-consuming and hard to capture at scale for large outdoor scenes. Fortunately, satellite imagery can provide a global geometric prior that is easy to access via public APIs. Cross-View Splatter fuses orthorectified satellite views with GPS-tagged ground photos to predict Gaussian splats in a unified 3D coordinate frame. By aligning ground and bird's-eye feature representations, our model improves scene coverage and novel-view synthesis, compared to ground imagery alone. We train on curated georeferenced datasets and paired satellite-terrain data, mined from open mapping services. We evaluate our method on a new benchmark for novel-view synthesis with georeferenced imagery allowing comparison to prior state-of-the-art methods. Our code and data preparation will be available at https://nianticspatial.github.io/cross-view-splatter/.

AI.tldr: Cross-View Splatter is a feed-forward method that fuses satellite imagery with ground-level photos to predict pixel-aligned Gaussian splats for outdoor 3D scene reconstruction, improving coverage and novel-view synthesis over ground-only approaches.
AI.motivation: Ground imagery is time-consuming and hard to capture at scale for large outdoor scenes; satellite imagery provides an easily accessible global geometric prior that can compensate for limited ground camera coverage.
AI.method: A feed-forward model that fuses orthorectified satellite views with GPS-tagged ground photos to predict pixel-aligned Gaussian splats in a unified 3D coordinate frame, aligning ground and bird's-eye feature representations.
AI.result: Improved scene coverage and novel-view synthesis compared to using ground imagery alone; evaluated on a new benchmark with georeferenced imagery against prior state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: Cross-view fusion of satellite and ground imagery is an effective and scalable approach for outdoor 3D scene reconstruction, enabling better coverage and rendering quality.

title: A Two-Parameter Weibull Framework for Diagnosing Transformer Weight Distributions
comment: 27 pages, 14 figures. Companion library npm-weibull-py and benchmark database available at https://github.com/tiexinding/NPM-Weibull-public
summary:
We apply the Weibull distribution -- a two-parameter family from extreme-value theory -- as a diagnostic framework for element-wise weight magnitude distributions in transformers. At initialization, i.i.d. Gaussian weights give |w| ~ HalfNormal, yielding k ~ 1.20 via middle-80% probability-plot fit (the protocol used throughout this work). This anchor makes k a principled, architecture-independent measuring stick for training dynamics; fitting each weight matrix independently at every layer at every checkpoint enables per-component, per-layer, and per-step diagnostics that aggregate statistics cannot resolve.
  Applying this framework to 12 model entries spanning 7 architectural families (Pythia, OLMo-1/2, LLaMA-3, Mistral, Qwen2.5/3) reveals three findings. First, FFN modules and the attention output projection W_o -- the Transmission Class -- fall in a narrow k band: median terminal k in [1.186, 1.204] across 12 entries (cross-family CV = 0.51%), shared across SwiGLU/GeLU activations, Pre-LN/QK-Norm placements, and 70M-14B sizes. Second, the attention input projections W_q, W_k -- the Selection Class -- depart from the Weibull family, with severity shaped by storage: separately-stored Q/K (OLMo-1, OLMo-2) yields k in [0.76, 0.99] (deep); GQA models yield k in [1.10, 1.16] (mild); Pythia's merged W_qkv occupies a transitional zone tracking training budget T/tau monotonically. Third, lambda grows substantially during training and scales with sqrt(eta/lambda_wd) within the Pythia family (Pearson r = 0.94, three Transmission kinds), directionally consistent with Fan et al. (2025). The two parameters carry independent information: k labels the functional class, lambda labels training progress.
  We release npm-weibull-py v0.4 (Python library) and DATABASE_v9_1 at https://github.com/tiexinding/NPM-Weibull-public .

AI.tldr: The authors apply the Weibull distribution (extreme-value theory) to analyze weight magnitude distributions in transformers, revealing that FFN and attention output projections form a narrow Weibull band (k≈1.19-1.20), while attention input projections deviate from Weibull behavior depending on architectural choices. The two Weibull parameters independently encode functional class (k) and training progress (λ).
AI.motivation: Existing weight analysis methods rely on aggregate statistics (e.g., norms, singular values) that cannot resolve per-component, per-layer, or per-step training dynamics. The authors seek a principled, architecture-independent diagnostic framework to understand how different weight matrices evolve during training across diverse transformer families.
AI.method: The Weibull distribution (two-parameter family from extreme-value theory) is fitted to element-wise weight magnitude distributions via middle-80% probability-plot fitting. Each weight matrix at each layer and checkpoint is independently fit. This is applied to 12 model entries across 7 architectures (Pythia, OLMo-1/2, LLaMA-3, Mistral, Qwen2.5/3), covering 70M-14B parameter sizes.
AI.result: (1) FFN modules and attention output projection W_o ("Transmission Class") show remarkably stable k in [1.186, 1.204] across all models (cross-family CV=0.51%). (2) Attention input projections W_q, W_k ("Selection Class") deviate from Weibull, with severity depending on storage format: separate Q/K (OLMo) → low k [0.76,0.99]; GQA → mild k [1.10,1.16]; merged W_qkv (Pythia) → transitional. (3) λ grows with training and scales with sqrt(η/λ_wd) (Pearson r=0.94, Pythia). k and λ carry independent information.
AI.conclusion: The Weibull framework provides a principled, granular diagnostic tool for transformer training dynamics. The two parameters serve distinct roles: k labels the functional class of a weight matrix (Transmission vs. Selection), while λ labels training progress. This enables architecture-independent comparison and reveals universal patterns across diverse transformer families.

title: DocQT: Improving Document Forgery Localization Robustness via Diverse JPEG Quantization Tables
summary: Document manipulation localization models achieve strong performance on public benchmarks yet fail to generalize to operational document workflows. We identify a critical and overlooked source of this gap: the mismatch between the narrow distribution of JPEG quantization tables used during training -restricted to standard libjpeg quality factors -and the heterogeneous compression profiles encountered in real-world insurance document pipelines. To isolate this factor, we conduct a controlled factorial study comparing two architectures with contrasting levels of quantization table awareness -FFDN [2] and Mesorch [20] -each trained under either standard quality factor augmentation (Standard-QT ) or operationally calibrated quantization tables sampled from DocQT, a quantization-table bank derived from a MAIF operational image corpus (Real-QT ), and evaluated under three recompression conditions. Training under Real-QT yields substantial localization gains on DocTamper [15] and significantly reduces the pixel-level false positive rate on authentic operational documents, but only for architectures that explicitly ingest the quantization table as input. The released DocQT quantization-table dataset and compression-reproduction material are directly available at https://github.com/Kyliroco/Improving-Document-Forgery-Localization-Robustness-via-Diverse-JPEG-Quantization-Tables. These results demonstrate that standard quality factor augmentation does not adequately proxy operational compression diversity, and that architectural choices explicitly conditioning on the quantization table provide a meaningful robustness advantage for real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies that document manipulation localization models fail in real-world settings due to mismatch between training JPEG quantization tables (limited to standard libjpeg quality factors) and diverse operational compression profiles. Training with realistically diverse quantization tables (DocQT) improves localization performance, but only for architectures that explicitly use the quantization table as input.
AI.motivation: Current document forgery localization models perform well on benchmarks but fail in real-world document workflows. The authors identify an overlooked source: mismatch between narrow JPEG quantization tables used in training vs. heterogeneous compression profiles in operational insurance pipelines.
AI.method: Controlled factorial study comparing two architectures (FFDN and Mesorch) with different quantization table awareness levels, trained under standard quality factor augmentation (Standard-QT) vs. operationally calibrated quantization tables from DocQT (a quantization-table bank from MAIF operational image corpus), evaluated under three recompression conditions.
AI.result: Training with Real-QT yields substantial localization gains on DocTamper and significantly reduces pixel-level false positive rate on authentic operational documents, but only for architectures that explicitly ingest the quantization table as input.
AI.conclusion: Standard quality factor augmentation inadequately proxies operational compression diversity. Architectural choices that explicitly condition on the quantization table provide meaningful robustness advantages for real-world deployment.

title: Don't Let Bandit Feedback Pull Continual LLM-Recommender Updates Off Target
summary: Generative LLM-based recommenders (LLM-Rec) require continual post-deployment updates, yet deployment logs provide only policy-shaped contextual bandit feedback: outcomes are observed solely for items exposed by a prior serving policy, inducing exposure bias and yielding partial, asymmetric signals consisting of relatively reliable positive responses and ambiguous no-responses. We propose an Anchored Bandit Policy Optimization (ABPO) framework for continual LLM-Rec updates that combines group-relative policy optimization (GRPO) with explicit treatment of exposure bias and feedback ambiguity. Specifically, we insert the exposed recommendation as a logged anchor into each GRPO rollout group, so that group-relative normalization is calibrated against the action actually exposed by the prior policy rather than against newly sampled rollouts alone. Because both positive- and no-responses are observed only through prior-policy exposure, we apply self-normalized inverse propensity scoring to the fixed anchor for both feedback types to correct for policy mismatch. At the same time, we treat the two feedback types asymmetrically in reliability: positive responses provide relatively direct endorsement signals, whereas no-responses remain ambiguous because they may reflect either true disinterest or unobserved external factors. To avoid overly aggressive updates from ambiguous no-responses, we temper their penalties with self-certainty, using the model's output-token confidence as a verifier-free reliability signal. Across five domains from Amazon Reviews and MovieLens, our method yields consistent post-update gains in recommendation accuracy while mitigating prior-policy-induced exposure bias more effectively than prior baselines.

AI.tldr: Proposes ABPO framework combining GRPO with exposure bias correction and asymmetric feedback handling for continual LLM-Rec updates.
AI.motivation: LLM-based recommenders need continual updates after deployment, but logs only provide partial feedback (exposure bias, ambiguous no-responses), making reliable policy optimization difficult.
AI.method: ABPO integrates (1) anchored rollups using exposed items as a baseline, (2) self-normalized inverse propensity scoring for bias correction, and (3) asymmetric treatment of positive vs. no-responses with self-certainty tempering.
AI.result: Across Amazon Reviews and MovieLens (5 domains), ABPO yields consistent post-update accuracy gains and better mitigates exposure bias vs. baselines.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling exposure bias and feedback ambiguity with anchored GRPO and asymmetric penalty tempering improves continual LLM-Rec learning.

title: A Nonlinear Complexity Index for Wearable PPG Cardiovascular Stability: Multiscale Validation, Systematic Evaluation Correction, and Bayesian Parameter Optimization
summary: Cardiovascular stability estimation from wearable photoplethysmography (PPG) requires a principled nonlinear framework, yet major gaps persist in heuristic parameter selection and evaluation protocols that inflate reported performance. We introduce a Stability-Constrained Cardiovascular Stability Index (SCSI) grounded in Cardiac Stability Theory and validate it across 176,742 segments from four heterogeneous PPG datasets at three temporal scales. Cross-dataset analysis demonstrates a large Kruskal-Wallis effect size (eta2 = 0.351, p < 0.001), strong cross-scale consistency (kappa > 0.97), and significant correlation with respiratory rate across 53 ICU records (Spearman r = 0.346, p = 0.011). We identify three evaluation artifacts that inflate heuristic AUC from a true baseline of 0.573 to 0.752: segment-level cross-validation leakage, test-set normalization leakage, and pooled-AUC overweighting that conceals per-patient failure. Correcting these artifacts and applying Bayesian optimization over 15 joint parameters yields SCSI with cross-validation AUC of 0.720. On 18 held-out records, SCSI achieves pooled AUC of 0.757 (95% CI: 0.686-0.828) and negative predictive value of 0.966 for tachypnea screening, while per-record AUC of 0.497 +/- 0.207 is disclosed for transparency. External validation on 42 elective-surgery records yields AUC of 0.621, confirming cross-population generalization. Ablation analysis identifies the nonlinear complexity module as the dominant component. A sparse three-component architecture is proposed as the minimal deployable configuration. The corrected protocol provides a reproducible benchmark for future wearable cardiovascular stability indices.

AI.tldr: Introduces a Stability-Constrained Cardiovascular Stability Index (SCSI) for wearable PPG, identifies three evaluation artifacts that inflate AUC from 0.573 to 0.752, and establishes a reproducible benchmark achieving 0.720 cross-validation AUC with disclosed per-record limitations.
AI.motivation: Current PPG-based cardiovascular stability estimation suffers from heuristic parameter selection and flawed evaluation protocols that inflate reported performance. The authors aim to establish a principled, reproducible framework.
AI.method: Proposes SCSI grounded in Cardiac Stability Theory, validated across 176,742 segments from four datasets at three temporal scales. Identifies and corrects three evaluation artifacts (cross-validation leakage, normalization leakage, pooled-AUC overweighting) using Bayesian optimization over 15 joint parameters.
AI.result: SCSI achieves cross-validation AUC 0.720, pooled AUC 0.757 with NPV 0.966 for tachypnea screening, but per-record AUC only 0.497±0.207. External validation yields AUC 0.621. Nonlinear complexity module is dominant component.
AI.conclusion: The corrected evaluation protocol and SCSI provide a reproducible benchmark. A sparse three-component architecture is proposed as minimal deployable configuration, with transparent reporting of per-record failures.

title: WBCAtt+: Fine-Grained Pixel-Level Morphological Annotations for White Blood Cell Images
comment: Accepted to Medical Image Analysis. arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2306.13531
summary: The microscopic examination of white blood cells (WBCs) plays a fundamental role in pathology and is essential for diagnosing blood disorders such as leukemia and anemia. To support further research on WBC images, multiple datasets have been proposed. However, they mainly annotate cell categories, and lack detailed morphological characteristics that pathologists use to explain their interpretations of cells. To address this gap, we introduce WBCAtt+, a novel dataset of WBC images densely annotated with 11 morphological attributes and five pixel-level cell components. With 113k image-level labels and 10k segmentation maps, WBCAtt+ is the first to provide comprehensive annotations for WBC images. Leveraging this dataset, we provide baseline models for attribute recognition and semantic segmentation. We also design an attribute recognition model to incorporate compositional structure of cells, further improving the recognition performance. Lastly, we showcase various applications enabled by our dataset, such as explainable AI models, including counterfactual example generation. \revision{The dataset and code are publicly available\footnote{https://doi.org/10.57967/hf/8143}}.

AI.tldr: WBCAtt+ is a new WBC image dataset with 11 morphological attributes and 5 pixel-level cell components (113k image labels, 10k segmentation maps), enabling attribute recognition, semantic segmentation, and explainable AI applications like counterfactual generation.
AI.motivation: Existing WBC datasets only annotate cell categories, lacking the detailed morphological characteristics that pathologists use to interpret cells, hindering explainable and interpretable AI in hematology.
AI.method: The authors introduce WBCAtt+, a densely annotated dataset with 11 morphological attributes and 5 pixel-level cell components. They provide baseline models for attribute recognition and semantic segmentation, and design an attribute recognition model incorporating compositional cell structure.
AI.result: The dataset contains 113k image-level labels and 10k segmentation maps. Baseline models and a compositional attribute recognition model are provided, with demonstrated applications in explainable AI including counterfactual example generation.
AI.conclusion: WBCAtt+ fills the gap in fine-grained morphological annotation for WBCs, enabling more interpretable diagnostic AI models. The dataset and code are publicly available.

title: Reasoning Portability: Guiding Continual Learning for MLLMs in the RLVR Era
summary: Vision-Language Models in Continual Learning (VLM-CL) aim to continuously adapt to new multimodal tasks while retaining prior knowledge. The emerging paradigm that couples Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) with Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) calls for a new pattern to guide continual adaptation. Advances in reasoning capability now make it feasible to impose constraints at the reasoning level. We formalize portability, a sample-level measure of how reusable the previous policy's behavior is on a new task, and empirically show that reasoning-level signals remain reliable on out-of-distribution samples while answer-level signals do not. We instantiate this as Reasoning Portability (RP) and propose Reasoning-based Dynamic Balance Continual Learning (RDB-CL), which modulates the per-sample Kullback-Leibler regularization in RLVR according to RP: a tight anchor preserves reusable reasoning on high-RP samples, while a relaxed anchor on low-RP samples permits exploration of new reasoning pathways. Experiments show that RDB-CL consistently outperforms baselines, improving Last accuracy by +12.0% over the vanilla RLVR baseline.

AI.tldr: RDB-CL improves continual learning in MLLMs by using reasoning-level portability signals to dynamically balance KL regularization, achieving +12% Last accuracy over vanilla RLVR.
AI.motivation: Existing VLM-CL methods rely on answer-level signals that are unreliable for out-of-distribution samples. The authors aim to leverage reasoning-level signals (more robust) to guide continual adaptation in the MLLM+RLVR paradigm.
AI.method: The authors formalize "portability" as a sample-level measure of policy reusability. They propose Reasoning Portability (RP) and RDB-CL, which dynamically adjusts per-sample KL regularization in RLVR: tight anchor on high-RP samples (preserve reasoning), relaxed anchor on low-RP samples (allow exploration).
AI.result: RDB-CL consistently outperforms baselines, with +12.0% improvement in Last accuracy over the vanilla RLVR baseline.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning-level signals are more reliable than answer-level signals for out-of-distribution samples, and dynamic balance based on portability effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting while enabling new learning.
